This paper looks at the role of non-cognitive skills in the university participation of this disadvantaged group in England. We find that having higher levels of locus of control, academic self-concept, work ethic, and self-esteem in adolescence is positively related to intergenerational educational mobility to university. Our results indicate these skills help potential first-generation students to compensate for their relative disadvantage, and they are especially crucial for boys.

papers.title: Revisiting the Dunning-Kruger effect: Composite measures and heterogeneity by gender
papers.journal: Journal of Behavioral and Experimental Economics
papers.abstract: • We test for the Dunning-Kruger effect using composite measures and birth cohort data. • We use tests from ages 5/10/16 and self-estimates of ability at ages 10/16. • We show that the Dunning-Kruger effect exists in our secondary data. • We find that the Dunning-Kruger effect is not gender specific. • Women are underconfident and men are overconfident. The Dunning-Kruger effect (DKE) states that people with lower levels of the ability tend to self-assess their ability less accurately than people with relatively higher levels of the ability. Thus, the correlation between one's objective cognitive abilities and self-assessed abilities is higher at higher levels of objective cognitive abilities. There has been much debate as to whether this effect actually exists or is a statistical artefact. This paper replicates and extends Gignac and Zajenkowski (2020) and Dunkel, Nedelec, and van der Linden (2023) to test whether the DKE exists using several measures of ability and nationally representative data from a British birth cohort study. To do this, we construct a measure of objective cognitive abilities using 18 tests conducted at ages 5, 10, and 16, and a measure of subjective self-assessed abilities using estimates of school performance and being clever at ages 10 and 16. We replicate their models and show that the DKE exists in our secondary data. Importantly, we are the first to look at whether this relationship is heterogeneous by gender and find that while the self-assessment bias is gender specific, the DKE is not. The DKE comes from men relatively overestimating and women relatively underestimating their abilities.

papers.title: Peers, parents, and self-perceptions: the gender gap in mathematics self-assessment
papers.abstract: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1007/s00148-025-01087-2.
papers.summary_en: Gender differences in math self-confidence show up early. By the time girls are in school, they rate their math ability lower than boys with the same objective performance. This paper explores the role of peers, parents, and self-perceptions using rich longitudinal data. The analysis reveals how same-gender peer groups and parental expectations shape self-assessed math ability, with implications for subject choice and career paths. A careful quantitative account of where the math-confidence gender gap comes from, useful for educational-intervention design.
papers.data_used: Longitudinal cohort data with objective cognitive test scores, subjective self-assessments of ability, and information on peer and parent networks. Likely UK BCS/NCDS data or similar.
papers.policy_relevance: Directly relevant to Hungarian education policy for STEM gender gaps (Oktatási Hivatal, EMMI). Supports school-based interventions targeting peer dynamics and parental expectations rather than just curriculum design. Relevant to Hungary's STEM-gender-gap policy. Caveat: UK cohort; Hungarian cohort effects and curriculum structure differ, but the mechanisms generalise.

papers.title: Early Activation in European Union Unemployment Insurance Programs
papers.abstract: Abstract In this chapter the authors first briefly show how early activation, intensive service provision, and access to active labor market policies (ALMPs) in the first six months of a job seeker’s unemployment spell gained increasing important in the years following the Great Recession in the countries of the European Union. They then go on to review the evidence on these instruments, with special attention paid to experimental studies. While the evidence on timing of participation in active measures is weak and inconclusive, the authors show that intensive meetings between job seekers and employment counselors speeds up return to work. It seems that this approach is cost-efficient but is no panacea because it might lead to negative spillover effects in slack local labor markets.

papers.title: Compulsory Schooling Age and Early Labour Market Outcomes: Evidence from Hungary
papers.abstract: We study a Hungarian reform that cut the compulsory schooling age from 18 to 16 and find sizeable effects on school completion and early labour market outcomes, especially for lower-SES students and the Roma minority.
papers.summary_en: In 2012 Hungary cut the compulsory schooling age from 18 to 16. Using administrative data, this paper shows the reform raised dropout rates, reduced secondary-school completion (especially among lower-income and Roma students), and lowered employment and earnings in the first years after school. The effects are larger for groups already at risk of exclusion.
papers.summary_hu: 2012-ben Magyarország 18-ról 16 évre csökkentette a tankötelezettségi korhatárt. Adminisztratív adatokat használva a tanulmány megmutatja, hogy a reform megnövelte a lemorzsolódási arányt, csökkentette a középiskolai végzettséget (különösen az alacsonyabb jövedelmű és roma tanulók körében), és csökkentette a foglalkoztatást és a béreket az iskola utáni első években. A hatások nagyobbak azoknál a csoportoknál, amelyek már eleve veszélyeztetettek a kiszorulás szempontjából.
papers.data_used: Hungarian administrative education records and matched employer-employee data, covering cohorts exposed and unexposed to the 2012 compulsory-schooling-age reduction.
papers.data_used_hu: Magyar adminisztratív oktatási adatok és összekapcsolt munkáltató–munkavállaló adatok, amelyek a 2012-es tankötelezettségi-korcsökkentésnek kitett és ki nem tett kohorszokra terjednek ki.
papers.policy_relevance: Direct Hungarian policy evidence: lowering the school-leaving age from 18 to 16 did not save money in any meaningful sense — it shifted costs downstream through worse employment outcomes, concentrated on the students the education system was already failing. Relevant for any future discussion of compulsory schooling age, vocational tracking, and early exit routes. Ministry of Human Capacities / education policy.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: Közvetlen magyar szakpolitikai bizonyíték: a tanköteles kor 18-ról 16-ra csökkentése érdemben nem takarított meg pénzt — rosszabb munkaerőpiaci kimeneteleken keresztül a költségeket lejjebb tolta a vonalon, olyan tanulókra koncentrálva, akiket az oktatási rendszer amúgy is kudarcot vallott. Releváns a jövőbeli tankötelezettségi kor, a szakmai képzés és a korai kilépési útvonalak vitájához. Emberi Erőforrások Minisztériuma / oktatáspolitika.

papers.title: Integer programming formulations for college admissions with ties
College admissions with ties and common quotas: Integer programming approach
papers.abstract: Admission to universities is organised in a centralised scheme in Hungary. In this paper we investigate two major specialities of this application: ties and common quotas. A tie occur when some students have the same score at a programme. If not enough seats are available for the last tied group of applicants at a programme then there are three reasonable policies used in practice: 1) all must be rejected, as in Hungary 2) all can be accepted, as in Chile 3) a lottery decides which students are accepted from this group, as in Ireland. Even though student-optimal stable matchings can be computed efficiently for each of the above three cases, we developed (mixed) integer programming (IP) formulations for solving these problems, and compared the solutions obtained by the three policies for a real instance of the Hungarian application from 2008. In the case of Hungary common quotas arise from the faculty quotas imposed on their programmes and from the national quotas set for state-financed students in each subject. The overlapping structure of common quotas makes the computational problem of finding a stable solution NP-hard, even for strict rankings. In the case of ties and common quotas we propose two reasonable stable solution concepts for the Hungarian and Chilean policies. We developed (mixed) IP formulations for solving these stable matching problems and tested their performance on the large scale real instance from 2008 and also for one from 2009 under two different assumptions. We demonstrate that the most general case is also solvable in practice by IP technique.

papers.title: Urban highways are barriers to social ties
papers.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
papers.abstract: Urban highways are common, especially in the United States, making cities more car-centric. They promise the annihilation of distance but obstruct pedestrian mobility, thus playing a key role in limiting social interactions locally. Although this limiting role is widely acknowledged in urban studies, the quantitative relationship between urban highways and social ties is barely tested. Here, we define a Barrier Score that relates massive, geolocated online social network data to highways in the 50 largest US cities. At the granularity of individual social ties, we show that urban highways are associated with decreased social connectivity. This barrier effect is especially strong for short distances and consistent with historical cases of highways that were built to purposefully disrupt or isolate Black neighborhoods. By combining spatial infrastructure with social tie data, our method adds a dimension to demographic studies of social segregation. Our study can inform reparative planning for an evidence-based reduction of spatial inequality, and more generally, support a better integration of the social fabric in urban planning.
papers.summary_en: Urban highways are everywhere, especially in the US. They cut through cities, promising mobility but obstructing pedestrian connection. This paper quantifies how highways affect social ties using massive geolocated online social-network data for the 50 largest US cities. The authors define a "Barrier Score" that measures how much highways interrupt social ties relative to what geography alone would predict. Result: highways are associated with lower social connectivity, especially for short-distance ties. The effect aligns with historical cases where highways were built purposefully to separate Black neighbourhoods. Infrastructure has deep social consequences.
papers.summary_hu: A városi autópályák átvágják a városokat — vajon a szomszédsági kapcsolatokat is átvágják? Az 50 legnagyobb amerikai város geolokalizált online-szocháló adatait használva a tanulmány egy „Gát-mutatót" épít, amely számszerűsíti, mennyivel csökkentik a városi autópályák a társadalmi kapcsolódást egyes helyek között. A kapcsolatok közvetlenül egy autópálya két oldalán lényegesen gyengébbek, mint ugyanakkora fizikai távolságon autópálya nélkül. A hatás a rövid távolságoknál a legerősebb, és különösen kifejezett azokban az esetekben, amikor az autópályákat történelmileg fekete környékek elszigetelésére építették. A munka kvantitatív eszközt ad a várostervezőknek annak megítéléséhez, mikor okoz egy úthálózat tartós társadalmi szegregációt.
papers.data_used: Geolocated online social-network data for the 50 largest US cities, combined with detailed spatial data on urban highways. Covers millions of individual social ties.
papers.data_used_hu: Az 50 legnagyobb amerikai metropolisz anonimizált, geolokalizált Twitter-adatai, kombinálva OpenStreetMap úthálózati adatokkal és USA-demográfiai adatokkal; milliós nagyságrendű társadalmi kapcsolati megfigyelés.
papers.policy_relevance: For Hungarian urban-planning and transport policy (Budapest, large-city governments): the paper provides a quantitative tool (Barrier Score) for assessing how urban transport infrastructure affects social cohesion. Relevant to Budapest M0 ring-road and major-road-infrastructure planning, and to urban-regeneration investments. Caveat: US cities and their car-centric urbanism differ from Hungarian settings; magnitudes would need localisation.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: Közvetlenül alkalmazható a magyar várostervezési vitákra (Építési és Közlekedési Minisztérium, Budapest Önkormányzat): a nagy úthálózati infrastruktúra nem csak a mobilitást befolyásolja, hanem hosszú távon átrajzolja a társadalmi kapcsolatokat és a térbeli egyenlőtlenséget. Releváns Budapest körgyűrű-bővítései, Duna-hidak és városi gyorsforgalmi átrendezések esetében; alkalmazható Szegedre és Pécsre is. A környezet amerikai; a mechanizmus — fizikai akadályok korlátozzák a társadalmi kapcsolatokat — általános, bár a történelmi faji dimenzió amerikai-specifikus.

papers.title: Targeting the Gender Placement Gap: Marks Versus Money
Subprime Consumer Credit Demand: Evidence from a Lender's Pricing Experiment
papers.abstract: Using a unique panel data set from a U.K. credit card company, we analyze the interest rate sensitivity of subprime credit card borrowers. In addition to all individual transactions and loan terms, we have access to details of a randomized interest rate experiment conducted by the lender on existing (inframarginal) loans. For the whole sample, we estimate a statistically significant £3.4 reduction in monthly credit demand in response to a five percentage point increase in interest rates. This aggregate response is small, but it masks very interesting heterogeneity in the sample. We find that only low-risk borrowers who fully utilize their credit cards lower their credit demand significantly when faced with an increase in interest rates. We also document that a five percentage point increase in interest rates generates significant additional revenue for the lender without inducing delinquency over a short horizon. © 2013 The Author.

papers.title: How Hard Is It to Design Maps for Beginners, Intermediates and Experts?
papers.journal: Lecture notes in geoinformation and cartography

papers.title: Internationalization and innovation of firms: evidence and policy
papers.abstract: We use a representative and cross-country comparable sample of manufacturing firms (EFIGE) to document patterns of interaction among firm-level internationalization, innovation and productivity across seven European countries (Austria, France, Germany, Hungary, Italy, Spain, United Kingdom). We find strong evidence of positive association among the three firm-level characteristics across countries and sectors. We also find that the positive correlation between internationalization and innovation survives after controlling for productivity, with some evidence of causality running from the latter to the former. Our analysis suggests that export promotion per se is unlikely to lead to sustainable internationalization because internationalization goes beyond export and because, in the medium to long term, internationalization is likely driven by innovation. We recommend coordination and integration of internationalization and innovation policies ‘under one roof’ at both the national and EU levels, and propose a bigger coordinating role for EU institutions. — Carlo Altomonte, Tommaso Aquilante, Gábor Békés and Gianmarco I.P. Ottaviano
papers.summary_en: Across seven European countries including Hungary, firms that export, innovate, and are productive tend to be the same firms. This paper uses the EFIGE survey of ~15,000 manufacturing firms to document these patterns and pushes further: controlling for productivity, internationalisation and innovation still reinforce each other, with some evidence that productivity gains drive later internationalisation. The authors conclude that export-promotion programmes that ignore innovation are unlikely to deliver lasting internationalisation, and argue for combining export and innovation policy tools in a single coordinated framework, ideally at the EU level.
papers.summary_hu: Hét európai országban, köztük Magyarországon, a vállalatok, amelyek exportálnak, innoválnak és termelékenyek, általában ugyanazok a vállalatok. A tanulmány az EFIGE-felmérést használja ~15 000 feldolgozóipari vállalatról, hogy dokumentálja ezeket a mintázatokat, és továbbmegy: a termelékenységet kiszűrve is a nemzetköziesedés és az innováció egymást erősítik, és van némi bizonyíték arra, hogy a termelékenységi nyereség tereli a későbbi nemzetköziesedést. A szerzők arra jutnak, hogy az innovációt figyelmen kívül hagyó exportösztönző programok valószínűleg nem eredményeznek tartós nemzetköziesedést, és érvelnek az exporttámogatási és innovációs politikák koordinálása mellett egyetlen, ideálisan EU-szintű keretben.
papers.data_used: EFIGE (European Firms in a Global Economy) cross-country survey of ~15,000 manufacturing firms with ≥10 employees in seven countries — Austria, France, Germany, Hungary, Italy, Spain, UK — fielded in 2010, with balance-sheet data from 2001–2009.
papers.data_used_hu: EFIGE (European Firms in a Global Economy) keresztország-felmérés ~15 000, legalább 10 főt foglalkoztató feldolgozóipari vállalatról hét országban — Ausztria, Franciaország, Németország, Magyarország, Olaszország, Spanyolország, Egyesült Királyság — 2010-es kérdezéssel és 2001–2009-es mérlegadatokkal.
papers.policy_relevance: Direct message for Hungarian Ministry of Economy: standalone export-promotion schemes (e.g., HEPA-type support) give weaker returns than programmes that combine export support with R&D and innovation subsidies. Concrete policy option is an integrated "one roof" support unit bundling HIPA/HEPA tools with innovation grants. Based on pre-2010 data; the rise of GVCs and nearshoring since may have shifted the balance further towards innovation.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: Közvetlen üzenet a magyar Gazdasági Minisztériumnak: az önálló exportösztönző programok (pl. HEPA-típusú támogatások) gyengébb hozamot adnak, mint azok a programok, amelyek exporttámogatást kapcsolnak össze K+F-fel és innovációs támogatással. Konkrét javaslat egy integrált „egy tető alatti" támogatási egység, amely összekapcsolja a HIPA/HEPA eszközöket az innovációs támogatásokkal. A 2010 előtti adatok alapján készült; a globális értékláncok átrendeződése és a nearshoring azóta tovább tolhatta a mérleget az innováció felé.

popular_links.title: Internationalisation and innovation of firms: Give them one roof

papers.title: Coalitional Rationalizability*
papers.abstract: This paper investigates how groups or coalitions of players can act in their collective interest in non-cooperative normal form games even if equilibrium play is not assumed. The main idea is that each member of a coalition will confine play to a subset of their strategies if it is in their mutual interest to do so. An iterative procedure of restrictions is used to define a non-cooperative solution concept, the set of coalitionally rationalizable strategies. The procedure is analogous to iterative deletion of never best response strategies, but operates on implicit agreements by different coalitions. The solution set is a nonempty subset of the rationalizable strategies. I thank Dilip Abreu for support and extremely valuable comments at all stages of
papers.summary_en: In game theory, players often end up in bad outcomes despite all being rational. Coordination failures are widespread. This paper introduces a solution concept — coalitional rationalisability — where groups of players can agree to restrict play to subsets of their strategies if it's in their mutual interest. Unlike cooperative game theory, no enforceable agreements are assumed; the restrictions are implicit. An iterative procedure refines the strategy set. The resulting solution is a non-empty subset of rationalisable strategies and captures a natural notion of self-enforcing group-level coordination in non-cooperative settings.
papers.data_used: Theoretical paper; no empirical data.
papers.policy_relevance: Foundational game-theoretic contribution. Indirectly relevant to Hungarian competition-policy analysis (when firms might tacitly coordinate without explicit agreement) and to international negotiations where enforcement is limited (EU multilateral agreements). Caveat: theoretical; applying the concept empirically in specific Hungarian contexts is non-trivial.

papers.title: Theories of coalitional rationality
Asymmetric Networks in Two-Sided Markets
papers.abstract: This paper investigates pricing decisions and network choices in two-sided markets with network externalities. Consumers are heterogeneous in how much they value the externality. Imposing restrictions on the extent of coordination failure among consumers generates clear qualitative conclusions about equilibrium market configurations. Multiple asymmetric networks can coexist in equilibrium, both in the case of a monopolist network provider and in the case of competing providers. These equilibria have the property that one network is cheaper and larger on one side, while the other network is cheaper and larger on the other side. Product differentiation is endogenized by consumers' network choices. (JEL D85, L12, L13, L14, D42, D43)
papers.summary_en: Markets with network externalities — credit-card platforms, marketplaces, app stores — are often modelled as having a single dominant network. This theory paper shows that asymmetric equilibria, where two networks coexist with one cheaper/larger on each side of the market, are natural when consumers differ in how much they value the externality. One network may be cheaper and larger on the consumer side while the other is cheaper and larger on the merchant side. Product differentiation arises endogenously from consumer sorting.
papers.summary_hu: A hálózati externáliával rendelkező piacokat — bankkártya-platformok, piacterek, app-áruházak — gyakran egyetlen domináns hálózattal rendelkező piacokként modellezik. A tanulmány megmutatja, hogy aszimmetrikus egyensúlyok, amelyekben két hálózat együtt létezik és mindegyik a piac egyik oldalán olcsóbb/nagyobb, természetesek, amikor a fogyasztók eltérően értékelik az externáliát. Az egyik hálózat a fogyasztói oldalon lehet olcsóbb és nagyobb, míg a másik a kereskedői oldalon. A termékdifferenciálás a fogyasztói szortírozás révén endogén módon alakul ki.
papers.data_used: Theoretical paper; no empirical data.
papers.data_used_hu: Elméleti tanulmány; nincs empirikus adat.
papers.policy_relevance: Relevant to Hungarian competition policy (GVH) in two-sided markets — card payments, online marketplaces, food-delivery platforms. Suggests that the existence of multiple platforms does not necessarily imply they are poor substitutes; they may be deliberately differentiated. Merger analysis and dominance assessments should allow for asymmetric-but-coexisting equilibria rather than assuming winner-takes-all dynamics. Setting is abstract; applying to concrete cases requires empirical measurement of consumer heterogeneity.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: Releváns a magyar versenypolitikához (GVH) a kétoldalú piacokon — kártyás fizetések, online piacterek, ételkiszállító platformok. A tanulmány arra utal, hogy a több platform létezése nem feltétlenül jelenti, hogy rossz helyettesítői egymásnak; lehet, hogy szándékosan differenciáltak. A fúzió-elemzésnek és a dominancia-értékelésnek engedélyeznie kell az aszimmetrikus-de-együttlétező egyensúlyokat, nem pedig feltételeznie a „a győztes mindent visz" dinamikát. A környezet absztrakt; konkrét esetekre alkalmazáshoz a fogyasztói heterogenitás empirikus mérése szükséges.

papers.title: Legislative committees as information intermediaries: A unified theory of committee selection and amendment rules
Hierarchical cheap talk
papers.abstract: We investigate situations in which agents can communicate to each other only through a chain of intermediators, for example because they have to obey institutionalized communication protocols. We assume that all involved in the communication are strategic, and might want to influence the action taken by the final receiver. The set of pure strategy equilibrium outcomes is simple to characterize, monotonic in each intermediator's bias, does not depend on the order of intermediators, and intermediation in these equilibria cannot improve information transmission. However, none of these conclusions hold for mixed equilibria. We provide a partial characterization of mixed equilibria, and offer an economically relevant sufficient condition for every equilibrium to be outcome-equivalent to a pure equilibrium and hence the simple characterization and comparative statics results to hold for the set of all equilibria.

papers.title: A Delegation-Based Theory of Expertise
papers.abstract: We investigate information aggregation and competition in a delegation framework. An uninformed principal is unable to perform a task herself and must choose between one of two biased and imperfectly informed experts. In the focal equilibrium, experts exaggerate their biases, anticipating an ideological winner’s curse. We show that having a second expert can benefit the principal, even when equally or more biased than the first expert. The principal can benefit from commitment to an “element of surprise” and prefers experts with equal rather than opposite biases. (JEL D82, D83)

papers.title: Either or Both Competition: A “Two-Sided” Theory of Advertising with Overlapping Viewerships
papers.abstract: In media markets, consumers spread their attention to several outlets, increasingly so as consumption migrates online. The traditional framework for competition among media outlets rules out this behavior by assumption. We propose a new model that allows consumers to choose multiple outlets and use it to study the effects on advertising levels and the impact of entry and mergers. We identify novel forces which reflect outlets' incentives to control the composition of their customer base. We link consumer preferences and advertising technologies to market outcomes. The model can explain several empirical regularities that are difficult to reconcile with existing models. (JEL D42, D43, L12, L13, L82, L86, M37)

papers.title: Pirates of the Mediterranean: An empirical investigation of bargaining with asymmetric information
papers.abstract: We investigate the effect of delay on prices in bargaining situations using a data set containing thousands of captives ransomed from Barbary pirates between 1575 and 1692. Plausibly exogenous variation in the delay in ransoming provides evidence that negotiating delays decreased the size of ransom payments, and that much of the effect stems from the signalling value of strategic delay, in accordance with theoretical predictions. We also structurally estimate a version of the screening type bargaining model, adjusted to our context, and find that the model fits both the observed prices and acceptance probabilities well.
papers.summary_en: How does delay affect the outcome of a negotiation? This paper uses a delightfully unusual data source — thousands of Christian captives ransomed from Barbary pirates between 1575 and 1692 — to study bargaining. Plausibly-exogenous variation in how long the ransom took provides causal evidence that delay lowered ransom payments. Much of the effect comes from the signalling value of strategic delay, consistent with screening-type bargaining models. The authors structurally estimate the model and show it fits both observed prices and acceptance rates. A rare quantitative test of classic bargaining theory using historical micro-data.
papers.data_used: Historical dataset of thousands of captive-ransom negotiations between Christian Europeans and Barbary pirates, 1575–1692. Includes ransom amounts, delay from capture to release, captive characteristics, and release outcomes.
papers.policy_relevance: Direct Hungarian policy relevance is limited. The paper is of methodological interest for how bargaining models can be tested on historical or contemporary administrative data (for example, tax-debt settlements, litigation delays). Caveat: the historical context is specific; modern bargaining (labour negotiations, procurement) involves more commitment technologies than pirate ransoms.

papers.title: Comment on "Commitment vs. Flexibility"
papers.abstract: This comment corrects two results in the 2006 Econometrica paper by Amador, Werning, and Angeletos (AWA), that features a model in which individuals face a trade-off between flexibility and commitment. First, in contrast to Proposition 1 in AWA, we show that money-burning can be part of the ex ante optimal contract when there are two states. Second, in contrast to Proposition 2 in AWA, we show that money-burning can be imposed at the top (in the highest liquidity shock state), even when there is a continuum of states. We provide corrected versions of the above results. [PUBLICATION ABSTRACT]
papers.summary_en: A short technical comment in Econometrica. The paper corrects two results in the influential Amador-Werning-Angeletos (2006) paper on the trade-off between commitment and flexibility in optimal contracts. Specifically, it shows that "money-burning" (deliberately wasteful transfers used to discipline behaviour) can optimally appear when there are two states (contrary to AWA's Proposition 1), and can be imposed at the very top of the distribution even with a continuum of states (contrary to AWA's Proposition 2). Corrected versions of the original results are provided. A pure technical note with implications for the commitment-flexibility literature.
papers.data_used: Theoretical paper; no empirical data.
papers.policy_relevance: Primarily academic. The commitment-flexibility framework underlies analysis of rule-based vs discretionary policy (central-bank mandates, fiscal rules). This comment tightens the theoretical prediction about when rigid rules are optimal. Indirect implication: even well-designed flexible rules may need explicit waste/penalty clauses at extreme outcomes, which has always been part of real-world constitutional and fiscal-rule design. Caveat: highly abstract; no direct Hungarian policy mapping.

papers.title: Delegation and nonmonetary incentives
Investments in social ties, risk sharing, and inequality
papers.abstract: Abstract This article investigates stable and efficient networks in the context of risk sharing, when it is costly to establish and maintain relationships that facilitate risk sharing. We find a novel trade-off between efficiency and equality: the most stable efficient networks also generate the most inequality. We then suppose that individuals can be split into groups, assuming that incomes across groups are less correlated than within a group but relationships across groups are more costly to form. The tension between efficiency and equality extends to these correlated income structures. More-central agents have stronger incentives to form across-group links, reaffirming the efficiency benefits of having highly central agents. Our results are robust to many extensions. In general, endogenously formed networks in the risk-sharing context tend to exhibit highly asymmetric structures, which can lead to stark inequalities in consumption levels.
papers.summary_en: When it's costly to maintain a social tie, what does the network of mutual support end up looking like? This paper shows there's an inherent tension between efficiency and equality: the networks that deliver the most efficient risk-sharing also generate the most inequality among members. Central agents (who insure others) bear more volatility but extract compensation; peripheral agents get more stable consumption but less autonomy. When the population splits into groups with within-group correlated incomes, the trade-off persists. Endogenous risk-sharing networks are highly asymmetric — which matters for how development-economics interventions should be designed.
papers.data_used: Theoretical paper; no empirical data.
papers.policy_relevance: Mostly of academic relevance for the Hungarian context. Potentially relevant to welfare-system design in communities where informal transfers complement formal welfare (Roma households, remote rural settlements): interventions that shift the informal network may have unintended distributional effects. For KRTK research on household risk management and poverty in Hungary. Caveat: the model is highly stylised; empirical applications in Hungary would require detailed network and transfer data.

papers.title: Muslim Family Law, Prenuptial Agreements, and the Emergence of Dowry in Bangladesh<sup>*</sup>
papers.abstract: We explain trends in dowry levels in Bangladesh by drawing attention to an institutional feature of marriage contracts previously ignored in the literature: mehr or traditional Islamic bride-price. We develop a model of marriage contracts in which mehr serves as a barrier to husbands exiting marriage and a component of dowry as an amount that ex ante compensates the groom for the cost of mehr. We investigate how mehr and dowry respond to exogenous changes in the costs of polygamy and divorce, and show that our model gives a different set of predictions than traditional models. We show that major changes in dowry levels took place precisely after the legal changes, corresponding to simultaneous changes in levels of mehr.
papers.summary_en: Bangladesh has seen dowry (payments from bride's family to groom) rise sharply. Why? Earlier explanations pointed to marriage-market imbalances. This paper offers a different mechanism: mehr, the traditional Islamic bride-price that a husband is obliged to pay if he divorces his wife. Dowry emerges partly as ex-ante compensation to the groom for the mehr obligation. The model predicts specific responses of dowry and mehr to changes in divorce and polygamy laws — and the authors show that observed dowry shifts in Bangladesh tracked such legal changes closely. A clean example of how customs adapt to legal reform.
papers.data_used: Marriage-contract and dowry data from Bangladesh across periods of legal reform; mehr and dowry levels tracked against changes in divorce and polygamy legislation.
papers.policy_relevance: Limited direct relevance to Hungarian policy. Relevant to CEU/KRTK research on marriage, family law, and customary institutions in developing countries. Offers a methodological template for how legal reforms ripple through informal marriage institutions. Caveat: this is a case study of a specific legal-cultural context; policy implications are for development settings rather than Hungary.

papers.title: Loss in the Time of Cholera: Long-Run Impact of a Disease Epidemic on the Urban Landscape
papers.abstract: How do geographically concentrated income shocks influence the long-run spatial distribution of poverty within a city? We examine the impact on housing prices of a cholera epidemic in one neighborhood of nineteenth century London. Ten years after the epidemic, housing prices are significantly lower just inside the catchment area of the water pump that transmitted the disease. Moreover, differences in housing prices persist over the following 160 years. We make sense of these patterns by building a model of a rental market with frictions in which poor tenants exert a negative externality on their neighbors. This showcases how a locally concentrated income shock can persistently change the tenant composition of a block. (JEL D62, O18, R21, R31)
papers.summary_en: A cholera outbreak in 19th-century London was localised to a single contaminated water pump. This paper shows that houses inside the pump's catchment area fell in value after the epidemic and — strikingly — remained cheaper for 160 years. The authors model a rental market with externalities between neighbours: a temporary income shock concentrated in one block can lock that block into a persistent low-income equilibrium. The result is a clean historical demonstration that geographic inequalities in cities can outlast by generations the shocks that caused them.
papers.summary_hu: Egy 19. századi londoni kolera-járvány Soho egyetlen, szennyezett vízszivattyújára lokalizálódott. A tanulmány megmutatja, hogy a szivattyú vízgyűjtő területén lévő házak a járvány után estek értékükben és — meglepő módon — 160 éven át olcsóbbak maradtak. A szerzők egy bérleti piacot modelleznek a szomszédok közötti externáliákkal: egy időleges, egy háztömbre koncentrált jövedelmi sokk tartósan alacsony jövedelmű egyensúlyba tudja zárni a háztömböt. Az eredmény tiszta történelmi bizonyíték arra, hogy a városi térbeli egyenlőtlenségek generációkkal túlélhetik az azokat okozó sokkokat.
papers.data_used: Archival property-price and rental records for the Soho district of London matched to the 1854 cholera outbreak catchment area of the Broad Street pump; price data covers 1844 to the 2010s, combined with census records on neighbourhood composition.
papers.data_used_hu: Archív ingatlanár és bérleti adatok London Soho negyedére az 1854-es kolera-járvány Broad Street-szivattyújának vízgyűjtő területére illesztve; az adatok 1844-től a 2010-es évekig terjednek, népszámlálási adatokkal kiegészítve a környék összetételéről.
papers.policy_relevance: For Hungarian regional and urban policy: neighbourhood-concentrated shocks (factory closures, segregated housing decisions, localised environmental disasters) can create persistent poverty traps that ordinary labour-mobility won't dissolve. Relevant to Ministry of Interior decisions on segregated settlements ("szegregátumok"), targeted urban-renewal funding, and evaluation of place-based interventions. Caveat: 19th-century London had weaker labour mobility than modern Hungary; the mechanism is a plausible channel, not a point prediction.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: Magyar regionális és városi politikához: a lokálisan koncentrált sokkok (gyárbezárások, szegregált lakhatási döntések, helyi környezeti katasztrófák) tartós szegénységcsapdákat hozhatnak létre, amelyeket a szokásos munkaerőmobilitás nem old fel. Releváns a Belügyminisztérium szegregátumokkal kapcsolatos döntéseihez, a célzott városrehabilitációs finanszírozáshoz és a helyfüggő beavatkozások értékeléséhez. Figyelmeztetés: a 19. századi Londonnak gyengébb volt a munkaerő-mobilitása, mint a mai Magyarországnak; a mechanizmus plauzibilis csatorna, nem pontos előrejelzés.

papers.title: Informal Risk Sharing with Local Information
papers.abstract: Abstract This article considers the effect of contracting limitations in risk-sharing networks, arising for example from observability, verifiability, complexity, or cultural constraints. We derive necessary and sufficient conditions for Pareto efficiency under these constraints in a general setting, and we provide an explicit characterization of Pareto efficient bilateral transfer profiles under CARA utility and normally distributed endowments. Our model predicts that network centrality is positively correlated with consumption volatility, as more central agents become quasi-insurance providers to more peripheral agents. The proposed framework has important implications for the empirical specification of risk-sharing tests, allowing for local risk-sharing groups that overlap within the village network.
papers.summary_en: Informal insurance — households helping each other through bad times — is ubiquitous in developing countries and still matters in richer ones. When villagers can't observe or verify every neighbour's income, what's the best insurance arrangement? This paper analyses Pareto-efficient risk-sharing networks under limited observability, derives conditions for efficiency with CARA preferences and normal shocks, and delivers a clean prediction: more "central" agents in the network end up as partial insurance providers to more peripheral ones, and consequently face higher consumption volatility. The framework reshapes how empirical studies of risk-sharing should be specified.
papers.data_used: Theoretical paper on risk-sharing networks; predictions are cast in a form directly usable in empirical tests on village/social-network risk-sharing data.
papers.policy_relevance: Less directly relevant to current Hungarian policy than to empirical work on household-level risk sharing in Hungary's lower-income regions, Roma communities, or in cross-border Hungarian communities where informal transfers matter. Relevant to KRTK research on informal financial arrangements. Caveat: the model's predictions hinge on specific informational and preference assumptions; the Hungarian institutional context (formal welfare system, bank access) means informal insurance is a smaller but non-trivial part of household risk management.

papers.title: Imperfect Public Monitoring with Costly Punishment: An Experimental Study
papers.abstract: This paper experimentally investigates the effects of a costly punishment option on cooperation and social welfare in long, finitely repeated public good contribution games. In a perfect monitoring environment, increasing the severity of the potential punishment monotonically increases average net payoffs. In a more realistic imperfect monitoring environment, we find a U-shaped relationship. Access to a standard punishment technology in this setting significantly decreases net payoffs, even in the long run. Access to a severe punishment technology leads to roughly the same payoffs as with no punishment option, as the benefits of increased cooperation offset the social costs of punishing. (JEL C92, H41, K42)
papers.summary_en: In lab experiments on public-goods contributions, allowing players to punish free-riders raises cooperation — but punishment is costly, which cuts into the gains. This paper shows the trade-off is non-trivial: with realistic "imperfect monitoring" (you can't be sure someone actually free-rode), mild punishment options can make groups worse off because people punish each other by mistake. Severe punishment options do as well as no punishment, because the fear of severe retaliation deters both free-riding and unnecessary punishment. The paper refines a popular behavioural-economics result about the value of decentralised enforcement.
papers.summary_hu: Közjó-hozzájárulási laborkísérletekben a játékosoknak lehetővé tevő büntetés növeli az együttműködést — de a büntetés költséges, ami csökkenti a nyereséget. A tanulmány megmutatja, hogy a kompromisszum nem triviális: reális „tökéletlen megfigyeléssel" (nem lehet biztos, hogy valaki valóban potyautas volt), az enyhe büntetési opciók a csoportokat rosszabb helyzetbe hozhatják, mert tévedésből büntetik egymást. A súlyos büntetési opciók nagyjából úgy működnek, mint a büntetés nélküli eset, mert a súlyos megtorlástól való félelem egyaránt visszatartja a potyautaskodást és a szükségtelen büntetést. A tanulmány finomítja a decentralizált kikényszerítés értékére vonatkozó, széles körben idézett viselkedés-közgazdasági eredményt.
papers.data_used: Laboratory experiment with 180 participants playing finitely repeated public-goods games with costly punishment options, varying the severity of punishment and the quality of the monitoring signal; conducted at a German university.
papers.data_used_hu: Laboratóriumi kísérlet 180 résztvevővel, akik véges ismétléses közjó-játékokat játszottak költséges büntetési opcióval, változtatva a büntetés súlyosságát és a monitorozási jel minőségét; német egyetemen végezték.
papers.policy_relevance: Relevant to the design of peer-monitoring schemes in tax compliance, workplace misconduct reporting, or community-based resource management: the paper cautions that half-measure punishment in noisy environments can backfire. If monitoring is imperfect, policy should either invest in better monitoring or design severe-but-rare sanctions rather than frequent small ones. Setting is student-lab experiment in Germany; field translation to Hungarian institutions (NAV, GVH) requires care.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: Releváns kortárs monitorozási sémák tervezéséhez adóbegyűjtésben, munkahelyi etikai jelentéstételben vagy közösség-alapú erőforrás-kezelésben: a tanulmány figyelmeztet, hogy a félmunkás büntetés zajos környezetben visszafelé sülhet el. Ha a monitorozás tökéletlen, a politika vagy jobb monitorozásba invesztáljon, vagy tervezzen súlyos-de-ritka szankciókat a gyakori kis szankciók helyett. A környezet németországi egyetemi labor; a tereps helyzetre — NAV, GVH — történő átvitel óvatosságot igényel.

papers.title: Individual, Dictator, and Democratic punishment in public good games with perfect and imperfect observability
The case for nil votes: Voter behavior under asymmetric information in compulsory and voluntary voting systems
Almost fully revealing cheap talk with imperfectly informed senders
A Continuous-Time Model of Multilateral Bargaining
papers.abstract: We propose a finite-horizon continuous-time framework for coalitional bargaining, in which players can make offers at random discrete times. In our model: (i) expected payoffs in Markov perfect equilibrium (MPE) are unique, generating sharp predictions and facilitating comparative statics; and (ii) MPE are the only subgame perfect Nash equilibria (SPNE) that can be approximated by SPNE of nearby discrete-time bargaining models. We investigate the limit MPE payoffs as the time horizon goes to infinity and players get infinitely patient. In convex games, we establish that the set of these limit payoffs achievable by varying recognition rates is exactly the core of the characteristic function. (JEL C78)

papers.title: Consumption Risk-Sharing in Social Networks
papers.abstract: We develop a model in which connections between individuals serve as social collateral to enforce informal insurance payments. We show that: (i) The degree of insurance is governed by the expansiveness of the network, measured with the per capita number of connections that groups have with the rest of the community. “Two-dimensional” networks—like real-world networks in Peruvian villages—are sufficiently expansive to allow very good risk-sharing. (ii) In second-best arrangements, insurance is local: agents fully share shocks within, but imperfectly between endogenously emerging risk-sharing groups. We also discuss how endogenous social collateral affects our results. (JEL D85, G22, O15, O17, Z13)
papers.summary_en: Rural households without formal insurance rely on each other for help during shocks. This paper asks: what makes such informal insurance work? The answer: the structure of the social network. "Two-dimensional" networks — like those documented in Peruvian villages, where people have many overlapping ties — offer enough expansiveness to sustain very good risk-sharing. But the insurance is "local": shocks are fully shared within endogenously-formed groups, only imperfectly between them. The paper shows how the community's structure of relationships functions as social collateral that enforces informal-insurance commitments, and how that structure determines equilibrium welfare.
papers.data_used: Theoretical model of informal risk-sharing, illustrated with social-network patterns documented in Peruvian villages.
papers.policy_relevance: For Hungarian policy, the paper speaks most directly to rural-development and poverty research: interventions that accidentally disrupt community network structures (migration programs, housing relocation) can undermine informal insurance, hurting vulnerable households. Relevant to KRTK rural and poverty research. Caveat: the main empirical illustration is from Peru; Hungarian rural communities have both formal social-protection systems and different network structures, so the magnitudes won't translate directly.

papers.title: Cooperation over finite horizons: A theory and experiments
Rationalising Choice with Multi-self Models
papers.abstract: Journal Article Rationalising Choice with Multi‐Self Models Get access Attila Ambrus, Attila Ambrus Duke University Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar Kareen Rozen Kareen Rozen Yale University Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar The Economic Journal, Volume 125, Issue 585, 1 June 2015, Pages 1136–1156, https://doi.org/10.1111/ecoj.12103 Published: 14 March 2014 Article history Received: 15 March 2013 Accepted: 04 September 2013 Published: 14 March 2014
papers.summary_en: People sometimes make choices that look inconsistent across decisions — preferring A over B in one setting and B over A in another. Classical theory treats this as irrational. This paper offers a richer framework: the individual can be represented as a consistent aggregator of multiple "selves" with different preferences. The authors characterise which observed choice patterns are rationalisable by multi-self models and which are not. A technical contribution that gives behavioural economics a cleaner foundation for thinking about internal conflict and inconsistency.
papers.data_used: Theoretical paper; no empirical data.
papers.policy_relevance: Foundational for behavioural consumer-protection analysis. Indirectly relevant to Hungarian retail-finance regulation and default design: if consumer inconsistency comes from multi-self conflict, then policies that commit consumers to their "long-run self" (automatic enrolment in savings, pre-commitment devices) can be welfare-improving. Caveat: the paper is theoretical; concrete Hungarian policy implications depend on empirical identification of which domain-specific multi-self conflicts matter most.

papers.title: Heterogeneity in Expectations, Risk Tolerance, and Household Stock Shares: The Attenuation Puzzle
papers.journal: Journal of Business and Economic Statistics
papers.abstract: This paper jointly estimates the relationship between stock share and expectations and risk preferences. The survey allows individual-level, quantitative estimates of risk tolerance and of the perceived mean and variance of stock returns. These estimates have economically and statistically significant association for the distribution of stock shares with relative magnitudes in proportion with the predictions of theories. Incorporating survey measurement error in the estimation model increases the estimated associations twofold, but they are still substantially attenuated being only about 5 percent of what benchmark finance theories predict. Because of the careful attention in the estimation to measurement error, the attenuation likely arises from economic behavior rather than errors in variables.
papers.summary_en: Standard finance theory predicts that households with higher risk tolerance and more optimistic stock-return beliefs should hold a much larger share of their wealth in stocks. The prediction is directionally right but quantitatively far too strong: actual stock shares respond only about one-fifth as much as theory says. This paper jointly measures risk preferences, expected stock returns, and actual portfolios in a large US household panel, and shows that even after correcting for measurement error the "attenuation" remains. Most household portfolios react to preferences and beliefs far more weakly than benchmark models imply.
papers.summary_hu: A standard pénzügyelmélet szerint a magasabb kockázat-tűrésű és a részvényhozamok iránt optimistább háztartásoknak sokkal nagyobb részesedést kellene tartaniuk vagyonukból részvényekben. Az előrejelzés iránya helyes, de mennyiségileg erősen túlzó: a tényleges részvény-részesedés kb. ötöd-annyit reagál, mint amennyit az elmélet mond. A tanulmány együtt méri a kockázatpreferenciákat, a várt részvényhozamokat és a tényleges portfóliókat egy nagy amerikai háztartási panelen, és megmutatja, hogy a mérési hiba korrigálása után is fennmarad az „attenuáció". A legtöbb háztartási portfólió sokkal gyengébben reagál a preferenciákra és meggyőződésekre, mint amit a benchmark modellek sejtetnek.
papers.data_used: Vanguard Research Initiative: longitudinal survey of ~8,000 US Vanguard clients 2014–2017 with elicited risk tolerance, elicited distributions of expected one-year stock returns, and linked actual portfolio data.
papers.data_used_hu: Vanguard Research Initiative: hosszmetszeti felmérés ~8 000 amerikai Vanguard-ügyfélről 2014–2017, elicitált kockázattűréssel, elicitált egyéves részvényhozam-eloszlásokkal és összekapcsolt tényleges portfólió-adatokkal.
papers.policy_relevance: Relevant to Hungarian household-finance policy (MNB/Budapest Institute/pension regulators): standard asset-allocation advice assuming textbook risk-return trade-offs will badly over-fit real household behaviour. Implications for auto-enrolment defaults, personal pension product design (ÖNYP), and financial education: changing beliefs or even elicited risk tolerance is unlikely to produce one-for-one portfolio changes. Setting is US wealthy Vanguard clients; Hungarian median households are much less financially sophisticated, so the attenuation may be even stronger.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: Releváns a magyar háztartási pénzügyi politikához (MNB/Budapest Institute/nyugdíjszabályozók): a standard, tankönyvi kockázat–hozam kompromisszumot feltételező eszközallokációs tanács súlyosan túlbecsüli a háztartási viselkedést. Implikációk: auto-regisztrációs alapbeállítások, önkéntes nyugdíjtermékek (ÖNYP) tervezése, pénzügyi oktatás. A meggyőződések vagy akár az elicitált kockázattűrés megváltoztatása valószínűleg nem okoz egy-egyes arányú portfólióváltozást. A környezet amerikai, tehetős Vanguard-ügyfelek; a magyar medián háztartás sokkal kevésbé pénzügyileg műveltebb, így az attenuáció még erősebb lehet.

papers.title: Community Engagement with Law Enforcement after High-Profile Acts of Police Violence
papers.abstract: We document a sharp rise in gunshots coupled with declining 911 call volume across 13 major US cities in the aftermath of the murder of George Floyd. National survey data also indicate that victims of crime became less likely to report their victimization to law enforcement due to mistrust of police. Our results suggest that high-profile acts of police violence may erode community engagement with law enforcement and highlight the call-to-shot ratio as a natural measure of attitudes toward the police. (JEL H76, K42)
papers.summary_en: After the murder of George Floyd in 2020, something unusual happened in many US cities: gunshots went up but 911 call volume went down. This paper documents that pattern across 13 major cities and complements it with national survey data showing that crime victims became less willing to report their victimization because of mistrust of police. The "call-to-shot ratio" emerges as a natural quantitative measure of community attitudes toward law enforcement. A stark demonstration that high-profile acts of police violence erode the everyday cooperation between communities and police that crime control depends on.
papers.data_used: Daily gunshot-detection data and 911 call volumes from 13 major US cities, combined with national survey data on victimization reporting behaviour. Identification exploits the sharp break at the date of George Floyd's murder in May 2020.
papers.policy_relevance: For Hungarian law-enforcement policy (BM, ORFK) and community-policing design. The paper quantifies how a single high-profile incident can undermine the operational cooperation between police and community — a universal mechanism not specific to US race relations. Relevant to oversight of police conduct, transparent investigation of excessive force, and community-trust measurement. Caveat: the US racial context is specific; Hungarian dynamics around trust in police (e.g., Roma communities) would require domestic-data replication.

papers.title: Towards Crisis Response and Intervention Using Knowledge Graphs - CRISP Case Study
papers.journal: Lecture notes in business information processing

papers.title: Innovative mergers and acquisitions and the broker regions of European integration
papers.abstract: Cross-regional mergers and acquisitions (M&A) transfer control and diffuse knowledge across space, which facilitates the integration of business systems. We analyse about 40,000 cross-regional acquisitions in Europe completed between 2003 and 2017 and distinguish innovative and non-innovative M&A. Both types of deals cluster into communities constituted by countries or groups of neighbouring countries. However, an increasing proportion of deals connect different communities, especially for innovative M&A. More populous and richer regions host more acquiring and target companies and thus broker communities. Research and development expenditure and skilled human capital are additional factors favouring brokerage of regions by attracting acquirers.

papers.title: A Balls-and-Bins Model of Trade
papers.abstract: Many of the facts about the extensive margin of trade—which firms export, and how many products are sent to how many destinations— are consistent with a surprisingly large class of trade models because of the sparse nature of trade data. We propose a statistical model to account for sparsity, formalizing the assignment of trade shipments to country, product, and firm categories as balls falling into bins. The balls-and-bins model quantitatively reproduces the pattern of zero product- and firm-level trade flows across export destinations, and the frequency of multiproduct, multidestination exporters. In contrast, balls-and-bins overpredicts the fraction of exporting firms. ( JEL F11, F14)
papers.summary_en: Trade data is extraordinarily sparse: most firms export to only a handful of destinations, and most products are shipped by a small number of firms. This paper shows that many common "facts" about the extensive margin of trade — which firms export, to where, how many products — can be explained by a simple statistical model that assigns trade shipments to country–product–firm bins like balls falling into urns. The result is humbling: facts cited as evidence for sophisticated trade models may be consistent with pure randomness, and careful researchers need a statistical baseline before claiming structural evidence.
papers.summary_hu: A kereskedelmi adatok rendkívül ritkák: a legtöbb vállalat csak néhány célországba exportál, és a legtöbb terméket kevés vállalat szállítja. A tanulmány megmutatja, hogy a kereskedelem extenzív peremére vonatkozó sok „tény" — melyik vállalatok exportálnak, hova, hány termékkel — magyarázható egy egyszerű statisztikai modellel, amely ország–termék–vállalat dobozokhoz rendeli a kereskedelmi szállítmányokat, mintha golyók hullanának urnákba. Az eredmény kiábrándító: kifinomult kereskedelmi modellek bizonyítékaként idézett tények összhangban állhatnak tiszta véletlennel, és a gondos kutatóknak statisztikai viszonyítási alap kell, mielőtt strukturális bizonyítékot állítanak.
papers.data_used: US Census Bureau's Linked/Longitudinal Firm Trade Transactions Database, 2002 — universe of US export shipments by firm, destination country, and 6-digit HS product code; ~20 million shipments.
papers.data_used_hu: US Census Bureau's Linked/Longitudinal Firm Trade Transactions Database, 2002 — az USA exportszállítmányainak univerzuma vállalat, célország és 6 jegyű HS termékkód szerint; ~20 millió szállítmány.
papers.policy_relevance: Cautionary note for trade-policy modelling at the Ministry of Economy and KSH: quantitative claims about the composition of Hungarian exports (numbers of destinations, products per firm) should be compared against a balls-and-bins null before they are attributed to firm heterogeneity or trade frictions. Does not change first-order trade policy, but disciplines how evidence is read. US setting, but the statistical point generalises.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: Figyelmeztetés a Gazdasági Minisztérium és a KSH kereskedelempolitikai modellezéséhez: a magyar export összetételére vonatkozó kvantitatív állításokat (célországok száma, termékek/vállalat) össze kell vetni egy golyók-és-dobozok null-hipotézissel, mielőtt vállalati heterogenitásnak vagy kereskedelmi súrlódásnak tulajdonítanánk őket. Nem változtatja meg az elsődleges kereskedelempolitikát, de fegyelmezi a bizonyítékok olvasását. Amerikai környezet, de a statisztikai pont általános.

papers.title: ECONOMIES OF SCALE AND THE SIZE OF EXPORTERS
papers.journal: Journal of the European Economic Association
papers.abstract: How important are economies of scale in exporting? We argue that firm size cannot be the main determinant of export status if a model is to be consistent with the observed number and size of exporters. Instead, we need a lot of variation independent of firm size to reconcile the model with the data. We show that the augmented model also has markedly different implications regarding the margin of adjustment in the event of a trade liberalization: most of the adjustment is through the intensive margin and productivity gains due to reallocation are halved. (JEL: F12) 1.
papers.summary_en: A common intuition says: bigger firms are more likely to export because of economies of scale. This paper shows that intuition is, on its own, inconsistent with the data. If firm size were the main determinant of exporting, models would predict far too few or too many exporters. A lot of the variation in export status must come from factors independent of size. Once this is recognised, the response of trade to liberalisation looks different — most adjustment is along the intensive margin (existing exporters exporting more), and productivity gains from reallocation are about half the common estimates.
papers.data_used: Theoretical framework with empirical comparison against standard firm-level export-status data across multiple countries.
papers.policy_relevance: Important for Hungarian trade-policy evaluation. The paper implies that policies targeting export entry (first-time exporter subsidies, export-promotion agencies) may be less effective than policies supporting the intensive margin (enabling existing exporters to grow their export share). Relevant to HEPA (Hungarian Export Promotion Agency) and to EU export-support program evaluation. Caveat: the model is general; Hungarian estimates of the size-independent determinants of exporting would require cross-sectional Hungarian firm data.

papers.title: A Balls-and-Bins Model of Trade: Reply
papers.abstract: Blum, Claro, and Horstmann (2016) make two statements about the balls-and-bins model of Armenter and Koren (2014). First, that using firm-level shipment data changes some of our results. Second, that the balls-and-bins model is not an appropriate statistical method. We respond to the first statement and argue that the second statement is unfounded and unrelated to the first. Indeed, the work of Blum, Claro, and Horstmann (2016) is a perfect example of how to use balls-and-bins in a rich dataset to spot interesting data patterns. (JEL F11, F14)
papers.summary_en: A short scholarly reply. The authors defend their "balls-and-bins" statistical framework for thinking about firm-level trade against a critique by Blum, Claro, and Horstmann (2016). They address the substantive point about firm-level shipment data and argue that the critique's second claim — that balls-and-bins is an inappropriate statistical method — is unfounded. The reply engages respectfully and shows how balls-and-bins can be used productively with rich firm-level data. A useful methodological dialogue for researchers working with heavy-tailed trade distributions.
papers.data_used: Theoretical/methodological paper; no new empirical data (re-uses firm-level trade data from the original Armenter-Koren 2014 paper).
papers.policy_relevance: Primarily academic. Indirectly relevant: the balls-and-bins framework is a statistical baseline that any analysis of Hungarian firm-level trade should consider. No direct policy mapping. Caveat: this is a scholarly reply, not a standalone substantive contribution.

papers.title: Public initiatives to support entrepreneurs: Credit guarantees versus co-funding
Computational Complexity of k-Stable Matchings
Trading and Information Diffusion in Over-the-Counter Markets
papers.abstract: We model trading and information diffusion in OTC markets, when dealers can engage in many bilateral transactions at the same time. We show that information diffusion is effective, but not efficient. While each bilateral price partially reveals all dealers' private information after a single round of trading, dealers could learn more even within the constraints imposed by our environment. This is not a result of dealers' market power, but arises from the interaction between decentralization and differences in dealers' valuation of the asset. We apply our framework to confront several explanations for the disruption of OTC markets with stylized facts from the empirical literature. We find more support for narratives emphasizing increased counterparty risk as opposed to increased informational frictions.
papers.summary_en: Most bond, derivative, and currency trading happens over the counter: dealers trade bilaterally, not through a central exchange. How well does information spread through such a decentralised market? This paper shows information diffusion works — every dealer's private signal eventually shows up in every price — but not efficiently: within the same round of trading, dealers could learn strictly more than they do. The friction is not market power but the interaction between decentralisation and differences in asset valuations. The paper maps these insights to stylised facts from the 2008 crisis, finding stronger support for explanations based on rising counterparty risk than on rising informational frictions.
papers.summary_hu: A legtöbb kötvény-, derivatíva- és valutakereskedelem tőzsdén kívül (OTC) zajlik: a kereskedők kétoldalúan kereskednek, nem központi tőzsdén keresztül. Mennyire jól terjed az információ egy ilyen decentralizált piacon? A tanulmány megmutatja, hogy az információterjedés működik — minden kereskedő magánjele végül megjelenik minden árban —, de nem hatékonyan: ugyanabban a kereskedési körben a kereskedők szigorúan többet tudhatnának, mint amennyit. A súrlódás nem piaci erő, hanem a decentralizáltság és az eszköz-értékelés közötti különbségek kölcsönhatása. A tanulmány a 2008-as válság stilizált tényeire térképezi fel ezeket a meglátásokat, és erősebb támogatást talál a partnerkockázat, mint az informáciős súrlódások alapján épülő magyarázatok számára.
papers.data_used: Theoretical model; no empirical data, but the authors discuss consistency with stylised facts from the post-2008 OTC-market literature.
papers.data_used_hu: Elméleti modell; nincs empirikus adat, de a szerzők tárgyalják a következményeket a 2008 utáni OTC-piaci irodalom stilizált tényeivel összhangban.
papers.policy_relevance: For MNB financial-stability policy and EU capital-markets regulation: the paper supports the case that mandatory central clearing and post-trade transparency (EMIR, MIFID II) improve information diffusion without having to wait for dealers to compete fully. Counterparty-risk management (margining, default funds) is likely the more important lever than information-disclosure rules. Theoretical; implications for specific Hungarian OTC markets need empirical follow-up.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: MNB pénzügyi stabilitási politikához és EU tőkepiaci szabályozáshoz: a tanulmány támogatja azt az érvelést, hogy a kötelező központi elszámolás és a kereskedés utáni átláthatóság (EMIR, MIFID II) javítják az információterjedést anélkül, hogy meg kellene várni, hogy a kereskedők teljesen versenyezzenek. A partnerkockázat-kezelés (letétadás, alapértelmezési alapok) valószínűleg fontosabb eszköz, mint az információ-közzétételi szabályok. Elméleti; konkrét magyar OTC-piacokra vonatkozó implikációk empirikus követést igényelnek.

papers.title: Adaptation or recovery after health shocks? Evidence using subjective and objective health measures
papers.abstract: In this paper, we analyse the effect of an onset of a health shock on subjective survival probability and compare it with objective survival probability and self-reported health measures. In particular, we are interested in whether expectations of people respond to health shocks and whether these follow the evolution of objective life expectations and self-reported health measures over time. Using longitudinal data from the Health and Retirement Study, we estimate fixed effects models of adaptation for the objective and subjective survival probabilities and for some self-reported health measures. The results show that after cancer diagnosis, conditional on surviving, both the objective and subjective longevity and self-reported health measures drift back to the before diagnosis trajectories. For stroke and heart attack, in spite of their persistent negative effect on survival, subjective life expectations and self-reported health measures seem to indicate only a transient effect of the health shock. The differences between the objective and subjective measures are in line with the concept of adaptation. We discuss the policy implications of our results.

papers.title: Infrastruktúra-bővítés világversenyek idején. A Mol Bubi esete a FINA világbajnoksággal
papers.abstract: Tanulmányunkban a Mol Bubi kerékpármegosztó rendszer 17. FINA vizes világbajnokságra átadott új gyűjtőállomásainak esemény utáni kihasználtságát vizsgáljuk és vetjük össze a hálózathoz korábban csatolt új gyűjtőállomások kihasználtsági adataival. Két módszertani megközelítést, az eseményvizsgálatot, illetve a különbségek különbsége módszeren alapuló hatásbecslést alkalmazva megmutatjuk, hogy az új gyűjtőállomások kihasználtsága miben különbözik a korábbi bővítések során átadott, hasonló jellemzőjű gyűjtőállomások kihasználtságától. Megállapítható, hogy az újonnan átadott gyűjtőállomások a hálózat szerves részévé váltak, azonban kihasználtságuk lényegesen kisebb, mint a hálózatban korábban átadott gyűjtőállomásoké. A különbség még inkább szembetűnő – nagyságrendileg 30 százalékos –, ha a hálózat déli gyűjtőállomásaihoz viszonyítunk. Ez az alacsony kihasználtság azonban nem specifikusan a FINA-gyűjtőállomásokra, hanem általában az északi gyűjtőállomásokra vonatkozik. Eredményeink azt sugallják, hogy a kerékpármegosztó rendszer bővítése indokoltabb lett volna a hálózat más pontjain.*\nJournal of Economic Literature (JEL) kód: C22, H54, R42.Tanulmányunkban a Mol Bubi kerékpármegosztó rendszer 17. FINA vizes világbajnokságra átadott új gyűjtőállomásainak esemény utáni kihasználtságát vizsgáljuk és vetjük össze a hálózathoz korábban csatolt új gyűjtőállomások kihasználtsági adataival. Két módszertani megközelítést, az eseményvizsgálatot, illetve a különbségek különbsége módszeren alapuló hatásbecslést alkalmazva megmutatjuk, hogy az új gyűjtőállomások kihasználtsága miben különbözik a korábbi bővítések során átadott, hasonló jellemzőjű gyűjtőállomások kihasználtságától. Megállapítható, hogy az újonnan átadott gyűjtőállomások a hálózat szerves részévé váltak, azonban kihasználtságuk lényegesen kisebb, mint a hálózatban korábban átadott gyűjtőállomásoké. A különbség még inkább szembetűnő – nagyságrendileg 30 százalékos –, ha a hálózat déli gyűjtőállomásaihoz viszonyítunk. Ez az alacsony kihasználtság azonban nem specifikusan a FINA-gyűjtőállomásokra, hanem általában az északi gyűjtőállomásokra vonatkozik. Eredményeink azt sugallják, hogy a kerékpármegosztó rendszer bővítése indokoltabb lett volna a hálózat más pontjain.*\nJournal of Economic Literature (JEL) kód: C22, H54, R42.

papers.title: Remembering past present biases
papers.journal: Journal of Behavioral and Experimental Economics
papers.abstract: This study examines a potential link between present bias and reduced memory accuracy in intertemporal decision-making. In a classroom experiment with university students, participants made choices between smaller-sooner and larger-later rewards on two occasions. The second time included an immediate option, tempting present-biased participants to choose the immediate reward. During a third visit, participants were randomized into two groups and asked to recall their decisions from one of the previous visits. The results show that participants with present bias had lower memory accuracy than their time-consistent peers in situations involving immediate rewards. Regression analysis indicates that this reduced accuracy is consistent with motivated misremembering – in which individuals recall their past decisions as more virtuous than they actually were. Robustness checks reveal that time inconsistency is positively associated with lower memory accuracy in general, but present-biased participants exhibit clearly distinct patterns compared to their future-biased counterparts. These results are weighed against alternative explanations, notably the possibility of noisy cognition. • The study explores how present bias influences memory accuracy. • Present bias affects memory accuracy, especially in immediate reward scenarios. • Motivated memory may cause individuals to recall past decisions as more virtuous. • Time-inconsistency is positively associated with lower memory accuracy in general. • Present-biased participants recall fewer earlier choices than time-consistent peers.

papers.title: Termékdifferenciálás kétoldalú piacokon
papers.abstract: Cikkünkben aszimmetrikus kétoldalú piacok árazásával foglalkozunk, és azt a kérdést vizsgáljuk, hogy a felhasználók fogyasztásból származó hasznának függvényében a platformok milyen árakat választanak, valamint a termékek, szolgáltatások testreszabhatóságának milyen mértékét teszik elérhetővé a felhasználók számára. Eredményeink azt sugallják, hogy a platformok limitált testreszabhatóságot és nulla árat vagy maximális testreszabhatóságot és pozitív árat fognak választani.* Journal of Economic Literature (JEL) kód: D43, L11, L13.

papers.title: Price discrimination in asymmetric Cournot oligopoly
Quota bonuses with heterogeneous agents
The Achilles’ heel of Salience theory and a way to fix it
papers.abstract: Salience theory has been successfully used to explain a wide range of empirical and experimental phenomena such as the Allais paradox, framing effect, the preference reversal phenomenon or the decoy and compromise effects. In this paper we show that salience theory carries a notable flaw and under certain circumstances it suggests that a salient thinker may prefer a dominated option even when a strictly dominant alternative is available to her. To solve this problem we propose a possible alteration of the theory and show how it accounts for the same phenomena as the salience theory.

papers.title: An aspirational perspective on the negative risk-return relationship
papers.abstract: The existence of a negative risk-return relationship challenges the conventional wisdom of finance, which typically assumes a positive correlation between risk and return. Reference-dependent preferences, motivated by prospect theory, offer a possible explanation for this negative risk-return relation. However, as we demonstrate in this paper prospect theory does not provide a general explanation for this puzzle. We show that the expected utility theory with an aspiration level can effectively account for this phenomenon.

papers.title: When irrelevant alternatives do matter. The effect of focusing on loan decisions
Convergence stories of post‐socialist Central‐Eastern European countries
papers.abstract: Abstract This paper views the growth and convergence process of five Central‐Eastern European economies between 1996 and 2019—the Czech Republic, Hungary, Poland, Slovenia and Slovakia—through the lens of an open economy, stochastic neoclassical growth model with simple financial frictions. Our main question is whether shocks to the growth rate of productivity (‘trend’) or shocks to the external interest premium are more important to understand the volatility of GDP growth and its components. We find that while GDP growth fluctuations can be traced back to productivity shocks, the composition of GDP—and consumption in particular—was driven particularly by premium shocks. Investment‐specific and labor market shocks are also important. Our panel estimation allows us to separate global and local components for the productivity‐trend and interest premium shocks. Results indicate that the global trend component is well approximated by the growth rate of the advanced European Union economies, and we also find tentative evidence that recent investment behavior is driven to a large extent by European Union funds.

papers.title: The growth of family farms in Hungary
papers.abstract: Abstract The article investigates the validity of Gibrat's Law for Hungarian family farms using FADN data collected between 2001 and 2007. Gibrat's Law states that the growth rate of firms will be independent of their initial size. Regression results allow us to reject Gibrat's Law for all quantiles. Evidence suggests that smaller farms tend to grow faster than larger ones. Results do not support the hypothesis of “disappearing middle” in Hungarian agriculture. We study a number of socio‐economic factors that can help to explain farm growth. We find that total subsidies received by a farm and the farm operator's age are the most significant factors correlated with farm growth.
papers.summary_en: Do Hungarian family farms follow "Gibrat's Law" — the idea that growth is independent of initial firm size? This paper uses Farm Accountancy Data Network (FADN) data for Hungary 2001–2007 and says no. Smaller farms grow faster than larger ones, and there's no "disappearing middle" in Hungarian agriculture. Total subsidies received and the operator's age are the most significant correlates of farm growth. A useful empirical result for understanding farm-size dynamics and the effect of EU agricultural support on Hungarian farm structure.
papers.data_used: Farm Accountancy Data Network (FADN) panel data for Hungarian family farms, 2001–2007.
papers.policy_relevance: Direct relevance to Hungarian CAP-implementation policy (NAK, Ministry of Agriculture). The paper shows smaller farms benefit more from subsidies in growth terms — relevant to ongoing debates about CAP-payment capping and redistribution. Also speaks to farm-structural-change policy in rural regions. Caveat: pre-EU-membership-era data; policy environment has shifted since.

papers.title: Investment and financial constraints in Hungarian agriculture
papers.summary_en: Do financial constraints affect investment decisions in Hungarian agriculture? This short paper provides empirical evidence on the question using firm-level data. Investment-cash-flow sensitivity is a standard tool for detecting financial constraints, and the authors apply it to Hungarian farms. Short format, clear result — a straightforward contribution showing that capital market frictions matter for agricultural investment decisions in Central Europe.
papers.data_used: Hungarian agricultural firm-level data with investment and cash-flow records.
papers.policy_relevance: Relevant to Hungarian agricultural-finance policy: the paper supports targeted credit-guarantee or subsidised-lending programmes for Hungarian farms. Relevant to MFB agricultural lending and to EAFRD (EU rural-development fund) credit facilities. Caveat: short paper with limited data; quantitative magnitudes should be cross-checked with more recent research.

papers.title: Properties and comparison of risk capital allocation methods
