As he adjusts his behavior in response, he lowers outcomes and hence becomes even more pessimistic about the fundamental, perpetuating the misdirected learning. The greater is the loss from choosing a suboptimal action, the further the agent's action ends up from optimal. We partially characterize environments in which self‐defeating learning occurs, and show that the decisionmaker learns to take the optimal action if, and in a sense only if, a specific non‐identifiability condition is satisfied. In contrast to an overconfident agent, an underconfident agent's misdirected learning is self‐limiting and therefore not very harmful. We argue that the decision situations in question are common in economic settings, including delegation, organizational, effort, and public‐policy choices.
papers.summary_en: What happens when an overconfident person tries to learn from experience? This paper shows that learning can be actively self-defeating. Overconfident agents systematically misinterpret outcomes: when things go badly, they blame external factors rather than their own choices, becoming too pessimistic about those factors. They then adjust their behaviour in ways that lower future outcomes and reinforce the misguided belief. The greater the cost of the wrong action, the further astray learning goes. The result is a new mechanism by which overconfident individuals (leaders, investors, entrepreneurs) can get stuck in self-confirming mistakes.
papers.data_used: Theoretical paper; no empirical data.
papers.policy_relevance: Relevant to any policy area where overconfident decision-makers act on feedback — financial regulation (retail investors), management training, entrepreneurship support. The paper suggests that feedback-based "learning" interventions have limited corrective power when overconfidence is present; more effective to structure decisions so correct information is implied by the action. For Hungary's financial-consumer education and SME-support programmes, this implies designing defaults and limits rather than relying on experience-based learning. Caveat: the model is theoretical, applicability depends on whether and how a given group is overconfident.

papers.title: Convergence in models of misspecified learning
papers.abstract: We establish convergence of beliefs and actions in a class of one‐dimensional learning settings in which the agent's model is misspecified, she chooses actions endogenously, and the actions affect how she misinterprets information. Our stochastic‐approximation‐based methods rely on two crucial features: that the state and action spaces are continuous, and that the agent's posterior admits a one‐dimensional summary statistic. Through a basic model with a normal–normal updating structure and a generalization in which the agent's misinterpretation of information can depend on her current beliefs in a flexible way, we show that these features are compatible with a number of specifications of how exactly the agent updates. Applications of our framework include learning by a person who has an incorrect model of a technology she uses or is overconfident about herself, learning by a representative agent who may misunderstand macroeconomic outcomes, and learning by a firm that has an incorrect parametric model of demand.
papers.summary_en: People learn from what they do, but if their model of the world is wrong, the learning itself can go astray. This paper establishes precise convergence results for such "misspecified learning" settings: the agent picks actions, sees outcomes, and updates. Even when the updating rule is correct given her (wrong) model, beliefs converge — often to systematically wrong conclusions. The framework covers examples like overconfident learners and people with incorrect mental models of technology. A technical foundation-building contribution that unifies several previous results in behavioural economics.
papers.data_used: Theoretical paper; no empirical data.
papers.policy_relevance: Foundational for behavioural-economics models that inform Hungarian policy (consumer protection, financial education, public-health communication). Caveat: highly abstract. Specific Hungarian applications require mapping the model's components (agent's mental model, observable outcomes) to concrete decision contexts.

papers.title: The equivalence of the minimal dominant set and the myopic stable set for coalition function form games
papers.abstract: In cooperative games, the coalition structure core is, despite its potential emptiness, one of the most popular solutions. While it is a fundamentally static concept, the consideration of a sequential extension of the underlying dominance correspondence gave rise to a selection of non-empty generalizations. Among these, the payoff-equivalence minimal dominant set and the myopic stable set are defined by a similar set of conditions. We identify some problems with the payoff-equivalence minimal dominant set and propose an appropriate reformulation called the minimal dominant set. We show that replacing asymptotic external stability by sequential weak dominance leaves the myopic stable set unaffected. The myopic stable set is therefore equivalent to the minimal dominant set.

papers.title: A teszteredmények társadalmi egyenlőtlensége és az általános iskolai szegregáció
The effect of decreased general training on skills and dropout - Evidence from a vocational school reform in Hungary
papers.abstract: The paper studies a unique education reform that decreased the length of secondary-level vocational education from 4 to 3 years, reducing the time spent on general subjects while keeping the time spent on vocational training. We use a difference-in-difference strategy by comparing reformed schools with early adopters before and after the reform. We find that students’ general skills have dropped considerably, but the probability of dropout has decreased, and the probability of getting a secondary qualification has increased. These results suggest that such a reform will have mixed labour market consequences, at least in the short run.

papers.title: A kompetenciaeredmények hatása a munkaerőpiaci sikerességre
papers.abstract: Tanulmányunkban nagymintás adminisztratív paneladatokon mutatjuk meg, hogy az országos kompetenciamérés 10. évfolyamos tesztpontszámai erősen összefüggnek a korai munkaerőpiaci helyzettel. Eredményeink szerint egy szórással jobb matematikai tesztpontszám átlagosan körülbelül 4-5 százalékkal, míg egy szórással nagyobb szövegértési tesztpontszám ezen felül további 1-2 százalékkal növeli a korai munkaerőpiaci kereseteket. A munkanélküliség valószínűségét is jelentősen csökkentheti a jobb kognitív készség: összességében egy szórással nagyobb matematikai tesztpontszám 1,5 százalékponttal csökkenti a munkanélküliségi esélyt, míg a szövegértési tesztpontszám növelése további 0,7 százalékponttal csökkenti. A kognitív készségek a magasabb végzettségűek esetében a keresetekre gyakorolnak nagyobb hatást, míg az alacsonyabb végzettségűek esetében nincs hatással a tesztpontszám a későbbi keresetekre. A munkanélküliség esetében minden végzettségi szinten van hatásuk a tesztpontszámoknak.\nJournal of Economic Literature (JEL) kód: I26, I24, J01.

papers.title: Tanári eredményesség és tanár–diák összepárosítás az általános iskolákban. Empirikus mintázatok három magyarországi tankerület adatai alapján
papers.abstract: A tanulmány egy új, Magyarországon egyedülálló adatbázisra épít, amely három tankerület nyolcadikos diákjainak kompetenciaeredményeit köti össze matematika- és magyartanáraikkal. A kapcsolás révén tanári eredményességet mérő, úgynevezett hozzáadottérték-modelleket becsülünk, majd ezeket felhasználva vizsgáljuk a tanár–diák párosítás mintázatait, vagyis azt, hogy különböző társadalmi helyzetű tanulók egyenlő eséllyel férnek-e hozzá magas hozzáadott értékű pedagógusokhoz. Azt találjuk, hogy a három tankerületben a tanári hozzáadott érték nagysága összhangban áll a nemzetközi irodalomban találtakkal: az átlagosnál egy szórásegységnyivel eredményesebb tanár az általános iskola utolsó két évében körülbelül 0,2 szórásegységnyivel növeli átlagosan a tanulók kompetenciateszt-­pontszámát. Összességében, illetve iskolák között nem találunk kapcsolatot a diákok társadalmi helyzete és az őket tanító pedagógusok eredményessége között, iskolán belül azonban az átlagosan jobb családi hátterű osztályt magasabb hozzáadott értékű tanár fogja tanítani matematikából. Ez az eredmény felhívja a figyelmet az iskolák tantárgyfelosztási gyakorlatának fontosságára, amellyel csekély mértékben ugyan, de valamelyest lehetne enyhíteni a minőségi oktatáshoz való hozzáférés egyenlőtlenségein.*\nJournal of Economic Literature (JEL) kód: I21, I24.

papers.title: Are separate classrooms inherently unequal? The effect of within-school sorting on the socioeconomic test score gap in Hungary
papers.abstract: This study investigates whether within-school sorting increases socioeconomic test score inequalities. Using universal test score data on 6-th, and 8th grade students in Hungary, we document the extent of within-school sorting in an institutional context where sorting based on ability or prior achievement is rare. We identify sorting schools as schools that systematically assign students with low and high socioeconomic status into different classrooms within the school. Then, exploiting school fixed effects and quasi-exogenous variation in sorting induced by enrollment and class size rules, we show that sorting has a significant and economically meaningful effect on test score inequalities between students from different socioeconomic backgrounds. Sorting harms low-status students, while high-status students gain much less, if anything, from attending sorting schools. We attribute our findings to the within-school reallocation of educational resources and differences in educational practices.
papers.summary_en: Even within the same school, students from advantaged and disadvantaged families often end up in different classrooms. Does this within-school sorting matter? This paper uses universal test-score data for 6th and 8th graders in Hungary. Exploiting school fixed effects and quasi-random variation in sorting induced by enrollment and class-size rules, the authors show that sorting has a significant and economically meaningful effect on socioeconomic test-score gaps. Sorting harms low-status students but helps high-status students little or not at all. A clear empirical case against within-school class-composition segregation.
papers.data_used: Universal-coverage administrative test-score data for Hungarian 6th and 8th graders. Exploits school fixed effects and quasi-exogenous variation from enrollment/class-size rules.
papers.policy_relevance: Directly actionable for Hungarian education policy (Oktatási Hivatal, EMMI). The paper provides evidence for Ministry of Education rules against within-school socioeconomic sorting: regulations that prevent class-composition segregation improve outcomes for disadvantaged students without measurably harming advantaged ones. Particularly relevant to Hungary's Roma-education inclusion efforts. Caveat: the effect is identified at a specific grade; long-run impacts into high-school and labour-market outcomes are for future research.

papers.title: Measuring the acceptability of EQ-5D-3L health states for different ages: a new adaptive survey methodology
papers.journal: The European Journal of Health Economics
papers.abstract: The acceptability set of EQ-5D-3L quantifies societal preferences concerning age and disease severity. Its measurement profiles and potential role in health resource allocation needs further exploration.

papers.title: Állami, önkormányzati, egyházi és alapítványi iskolák: részarányok, tanulói összetétel és tanulói teljesítmények
Állami, önkormányzati, egyházi és alapítványi iskolák
Immigrant–native pay gap driven by lack of access to high-paying jobs
papers.abstract: Immigrants to high-income countries often face considerable and persistent difficulties in the labour market<sup>1-6</sup>, whereas their native-born children typically experience economic progress<sup>6-9</sup>. However, little is known about the extent to which these immigrant-native earnings differences stem from unequal pay when doing the same work for the same employer versus labour market processes that sort immigrants into lower-paid jobs. Here, using data from nine European and North American countries, we show that the segregation of workers with immigrant backgrounds into lower-paying jobs accounts for about three-quarters of overall immigrant-native earnings differences. Although within-job pay inequality remains notable for immigrants in several countries, our results demonstrate that unequal access to higher-paying jobs is the primary driver of the immigrant-native pay gap across a range of institutionally and demographically diverse contexts. These findings highlight the importance of policies aimed at reducing between-job segregation, such as language training<sup>10-13</sup>, job training<sup>13-15</sup>, job search assistance programmes<sup>13,15</sup>, improving access to domestic education<sup>13,16,17</sup>, recognizing foreign qualifications<sup>18,19</sup>, and settlement programmes aimed at enhancing access to job-relevant information and networks<sup>13,20,21</sup>. Policies that target employer bias in hiring and promotion decisions are also likely to be effective, whereas measures aimed at ensuring equal pay for equal work may have more limited scope for further progress in closing the immigrant-native pay gap<sup>22-28</sup>.
papers.summary_en: Why do immigrants earn less than natives in rich countries? This paper uses linked employer-employee data from nine European and North American countries to separate two channels: (a) unequal pay for the same work at the same employer, and (b) immigrants being sorted into lower-paying jobs. The striking result: job sorting accounts for about three-quarters of the immigrant-native earnings gap. Within-job pay inequality exists and matters, but unequal access to higher-paying employers and positions is the dominant driver. Policy implications shift accordingly: addressing hiring and promotion barriers matters more than equal-pay enforcement.
papers.data_used: Linked employer-employee administrative data from 9 countries (including Hungary, via boza-istvan). Covers 2 billion+ job-years nested within 50 million+ workplace-years. Enables decomposition of earnings gaps into within-job and between-job components.
papers.policy_relevance: Highly relevant for Hungarian integration policy. The paper shows that closing immigrant-native pay gaps requires tackling job-access barriers — credential recognition, language programs, occupation licensing, network-based hiring — more than wage-discrimination enforcement. Relevant to ETDA, BM migration policy, and employer-level diversity efforts. Hungary-specific estimates (via Boza's KRTK work) are embedded in the sample. Caveat: sample is 9 countries; Hungarian context includes specific Roma-employment barriers that are a different case from typical immigrant-native dynamics.

papers.title: Sectoral Technology and Structural Transformation
papers.abstract: We assess how the properties of technology affect structural transformation, i.e., the reallocation of production factors across the broad sectors of agriculture, manufacturing, and services. To this end, we estimate sectoral constant elasticity of substitution (CES) and Cobb-Douglas production functions on postwar US data. We find that differences in technical progress across the three sectors are the dominant force behind structural transformation whereas other differences across sectoral technology are of second-order importance. Our findings imply that Cobb-Douglas sectoral production functions that differ only in technical progress capture the main technological forces behind the postwar US structural transformation. (JEL E16, E25, O33, O47)
papers.summary_en: Why do economies reallocate labour out of agriculture, then manufacturing, and into services as they develop? This paper fits sectoral production functions to postwar US data and finds that differences in technical progress across sectors are the dominant driver of structural transformation — not differences in capital intensity, substitution elasticities, or input requirements. The implication: a simple model with Cobb–Douglas sectoral production functions and sector-specific technical progress captures most of the transformation dynamics. Useful for anyone calibrating multi-sector growth models for developing or transition economies.
papers.summary_hu: Miért allokálják át a gazdaságok a munkát a mezőgazdaságból, majd a feldolgozóiparból, a szolgáltatásokba, ahogy fejlődnek? A tanulmány szektorális termelési függvényeket illeszt háború utáni amerikai adatokra, és azt találja, hogy a szektorok közötti technológiai haladás különbségei a strukturális átalakulás domináns mozgatórugói — nem a tőke-intenzitás, helyettesíthetőségi rugalmasságok vagy input-követelmények különbségei. Következmény: egy egyszerű modell Cobb–Douglas szektor-termelési függvényekkel és szektor-specifikus technológiai haladással a legtöbb átalakulási dinamikát megragadja. Hasznos mindenkinek, aki többsz szektorú növekedési modelleket kalibrál fejlődő vagy átmeneti gazdaságokra.
papers.data_used: US postwar sectoral data, 1947–2010: value added and factor inputs for agriculture, manufacturing, and services from the BEA and BLS; supplemented with input-output tables.
papers.data_used_hu: Amerikai háború utáni szektorális adatok, 1947–2010: hozzáadott érték és tényező-inputok a mezőgazdaságra, feldolgozóiparra és szolgáltatásokra a BEA-tól és BLS-től; input-output táblákkal kiegészítve.
papers.policy_relevance: For Hungarian macro-diagnostic and long-run-growth discussions: the paper supports a view of structural change as technology-driven, which implies Hungarian deindustrialisation since the 1990s is largely natural, not a policy failure. Relevant framing for Ministry of Economy debates on "reindustrialisation" policies — such policies face long-run technology headwinds. Uses US data; Hungarian technology paths differ but the conceptual framework transfers.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: A magyar makro-diagnosztikai és hosszú távú növekedési vitákhoz: a tanulmány támogatja a strukturális változás mint technológia-vezérelt nézetet, ami azt implikálja, hogy a magyar deindusztrializáció az 1990-es évek óta nagyrészt természetes, nem szakpolitikai kudarc. Releváns keret a Gazdasági Minisztérium „reindusztrializáció" vitáihoz — az ilyen politikák hosszú távú technológiai ellenszélnek néznek elébe. Amerikai adatot használ; a magyar technológia-pályák különböznek, de a fogalmi keret átvihető.

papers.title: Two Perspectives on Preferences and Structural Transformation
papers.abstract: We assess the empirical importance of changes in income and relative prices for structural transformation in the postwar United States. We explain two natural approaches to the data: sectors may be categories of final expenditure or value added; e.g., the service sector may be the final expenditure on services or the value added from service industries. We estimate preferences for each approach and find that with final expenditure income effects are the dominant force behind structural transformation, whereas with value-added categories price effects are more important. We show how the input-output structure of the United States can reconcile these findings. (JEL E21, L16)
papers.summary_en: Is structural transformation driven by income (as people get richer, they demand relatively more services) or by prices (services become relatively more expensive)? The answer depends on how you slice the data. Using postwar US data, the paper shows that with "final-expenditure" definitions of sectors, income effects dominate; with "value-added" definitions, price effects dominate. The two findings are reconciled by the input–output structure of the economy. The paper is a careful methodological correction to a long-running debate.
papers.summary_hu: A strukturális átalakulást a jövedelem vezérli (ahogy az emberek gazdagabbak lesznek, relatíve többet kérnek szolgáltatásokból), vagy az árak (a szolgáltatások relatíve drágábbak lesznek)? A válasz attól függ, hogyan bontjuk meg az adatokat. Háború utáni amerikai adatokat használva a tanulmány megmutatja, hogy a szektorok „végső-kiadás" meghatározásával a jövedelmi hatások dominálnak; a „hozzáadott érték" meghatározással az ár-hatások dominálnak. A két találat összebékíthető a gazdaság input–output struktúráján át. A tanulmány gondos módszertani javítást ad egy régóta tartó vitához.
papers.data_used: US sectoral data 1947–2010 from BEA national accounts and BLS industry statistics, with final-expenditure and value-added decompositions of agriculture, manufacturing, and services; matched to consumer-price and producer-price indices.
papers.data_used_hu: Amerikai szektorális adatok 1947–2010 a BEA nemzeti számlákból és BLS ipari statisztikákból, végső-kiadás és hozzáadott érték dekompozícióval a mezőgazdaságra, feldolgozóiparra és szolgáltatásokra; fogyasztói-ár és termelői-ár indexekhez illesztve.
papers.policy_relevance: Methodologically important for Hungarian Ministry of Economy staff working on structural-transformation and productivity diagnostics: choice between final-expenditure and value-added sector definitions materially affects the conclusions. Affects how deindustrialisation narratives are told and how policy levers (income-elasticity-sensitive vs relative-price-sensitive) are chosen. US setting; the methodological caveat is universal.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: Módszertanilag fontos a magyar Gazdasági Minisztérium strukturális-átalakulási és termelékenységi diagnosztikával foglalkozó munkatársainak: a végső-kiadás vs. hozzáadott érték szektor-meghatározás közötti választás érdemben befolyásolja a következtetéseket. Befolyásolja, hogyan meséljük el a deindusztrializáció narratíváját és hogyan választjuk ki a szakpolitikai eszközöket (jövedelem-rugalmasságra vagy relatív-árra érzékeny). Amerikai környezet; a módszertani figyelmeztetés általános.

papers.title: Growth and Structural Transformation
Structural Change in Investment and Consumption—A Unified Analysis
papers.abstract: Abstract The structural-change literature typically assumes that investment is produced in manufacturing. We establish that this assumption is counterfactual: in the postwar U.S., the share of services value added in investment expenditure has been steadily growing. We develop a new model that features structural change in investment and consumption, characterize its equilibrium properties, and provide empirical support for it. We establish that modelling structural change in investment leads to three novel insights: constant TFP growth in all sectors is inconsistent with the existence of aggregate balanced growth with structural change; the sector with the slowest TFP growth absorbs all resources asymptotically; technical change is endogenously investment-biased.
papers.summary_en: The long-standing story of structural change — labour moving from agriculture to manufacturing to services — has mostly focused on consumption. This paper adds the missing piece: structural change in investment. Using post-war US data, the authors show that services' share of investment expenditure has been steadily growing. They then build and empirically support a unified model of structural change in both consumption and investment. Three new lessons: balanced growth with structural change requires different TFP growth rates across sectors; the slowest-growing sector absorbs all resources asymptotically; and technical change turns out to be endogenously biased toward investment-goods sectors. A conceptually important reframing of growth accounting.
papers.data_used: Post-war US sectoral data: value-added and investment-expenditure shares across goods and services sectors, TFP growth by sector.
papers.policy_relevance: For Hungarian long-run productivity accounting and GDP-growth projections (MNB, PM), the paper reshapes how to think about which sectors drive growth. Services now matter for investment, not just for consumption. Directly relevant to fiscal-space projections and to whether industrial-policy emphasis on manufacturing investment still makes sense. Caveat: the empirical basis is US; Hungarian structural-change paths differ (still more industrial than comparable Western economies), so the quantitative story would need Hungarian calibration before informing specific policy.

papers.title: Determinacy through intertemporal capital adjustment costs
On the stability of the two-sector neoclassical growth model with externalities
papers.journal: Journal of Economic Dynamics and Control

papers.title: Endogenous sector–biased technological change and industrial policy
papers.abstract: We build a model of structural transformation with endogenous sector–biased technological change. We show that if the return to specialization is larger in the goods sector than in the service sector, then the equilibrium has the following properties: aggregate growth is balanced; the service sector receives more innovation but the goods sector experiences more productivity growth; structural transformation takes place from goods to services. Compared to the efficient allocation the laissez–faire equilibrium has too much labor in the goods sector, implying that optimal industrial policy should aim to increase, not decrease, the pace of structural transformation.

papers.title: Ruling Out Multiplicity and Indeterminacy: The Role of Heterogeneity
papers.abstract: It is well known that economies of scale that are external to the individual decision makers can lead to self-fulfilling prophecies and the multiplicity or even indeterminacy of equilibrium. We argue that the importance of this source of multiplicity and indeterminacy is overstated in representative agent models, as they ignore the potential stabilizing effect of heterogeneity. We illustrate this in a version of Matsuyama's (1991) two-sector model with increasing returns to scale. Two main results are shown. First, sufficient homogeneity with respect to individual productivity leads to the instability and non-uniqueness of a given stationary state and the indeterminacy of the corresponding stationary state equilibrium. Second, sufficient heterogeneity leads to the global saddle-path stability and the uniqueness of a given stationary state and the global uniqueness of equilibrium.

papers.title: Defining participatory video from practice
Cross-border mergers and acquisitions and the role of trade costs
Early tracking and competition – A recipe for major inequalities in Hungary
Gender differences in preferences of adolescents: Evidence from a large-scale classroom experiment
papers.abstract: In this study, we estimate unadjusted and adjusted gender gaps in time preference, risk attitudes, altruism, trust, trustworthiness, cooperation, and competitiveness using data on 1088 high school students from 53 classes. These data, collected by running incentivized experiments in Hungarian classrooms, are linked to an administrative data source on the students’ standardized test scores, grades and family background. After taking into account class fixed effects, we find that females are significantly more altruistic, but are less present biased, less risk tolerant, less trusting, less trustworthy, and less competitive than males. At the same time we do not observe significant gender differences in patience, time inconsistency and cooperation at the 5% significance level. We also show that most of these initial gender differences do not change even if we control for age, family background, cognitive skills and school grades in a regression framework. We risk over-control when we include the time spent on each task as well as the other preference domains in our regressions, but the gender gap remains significant in social preferences (altruism, trust and trustworthiness), present bias and competition.
papers.summary_en: Where do gender gaps in preferences show up among adolescents? Using incentivized classroom experiments with 1,088 Hungarian high-school students linked to administrative records, this paper measures time, risk, altruism, trust, cooperation, and competitiveness. Adjusting for class fixed effects, females are significantly more altruistic but less present-biased, less risk-tolerant, less trusting, less trustworthy, and less competitive than males. Controlling for age, family background, cognitive skills, and grades does not eliminate these gaps. The paper documents which preference dimensions are gendered at this age — important for understanding how gender gaps emerge later in careers.
papers.summary_hu: Hol mutatkoznak nemi szakadékok a preferenciákban a kamaszok körében? 1 088 magyar középiskolás ösztönzött osztálytermi kísérleteit és adminisztratív rekordokkal összekapcsolva a tanulmány méri az idő-, kockázati-, altruisztikus-, bizalom-, kooperációs- és versengési-preferenciákat. Osztály fixhatásokra kiigazítva a lányok szignifikánsan altruistább, de kevésbé jelenhajlamos, kevésbé kockázattoleráns, kevésbé bizalmas, kevésbé megbízható és kevésbé versengő, mint a fiúk. Az életkor, családi háttér, kognitív képességek és jegyek kontrollálása után sem szűnnek meg ezek a szakadékok. A tanulmány dokumentálja, mely preferencia-dimenziók nemi meghatározottságúak ebben az életkorban — fontos annak megértéséhez, hogyan alakulnak ki a nemi szakadékok később a karrierekben.
papers.data_used: Incentivized classroom experiments with 1,088 high-school students across 53 Hungarian classrooms, linked to administrative records (standardized test scores, grades, family background).
papers.data_used_hu: Ösztönzött osztálytermi kísérletek 1 088 középiskolás diákkal 53 magyar osztályban, adminisztratív rekordokhoz (szabványosított teszt-pontszámok, jegyek, családi háttér) kapcsolva.
papers.policy_relevance: For Hungarian education policy: the paper provides Hungarian-specific evidence on adolescent gender-preference gaps, which is input to STEM-track-encouragement programmes, career-counselling design, and competitive admission-exam design. The large gender gap in competitiveness at age 15–18 is directly relevant to debates about how competitive vs non-competitive school-admission systems amplify or dampen gender effects on educational trajectories.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: A magyar oktatáspolitikához: a tanulmány magyar-specifikus bizonyítékot ad a kamaszkori nemi-preferencia szakadékokról, ami bemenet a STEM-pálya ösztönző programokhoz, pályatanácsadási tervezéshez és a versenyző felvételi vizsga-tervezéshez. A 15–18 éves kori versengésbeli nagy nemi szakadék közvetlenül releváns ahhoz, hogyan erősítik vagy tompítják a versenyző vs. nem-versenyző iskolai-felvételi rendszerek a nemi hatásokat az oktatási pályákon.

papers.title: Bevándorlók kulturális integrációját meghatározó tényezők
Per-Shipment Costs and the Lumpiness of International Trade
papers.journal: The Review of Economics and Statistics
papers.abstract: Using detailed U.S. and Spanish export data, we document that trade costs of a per-shipment nature are associated with less frequent and larger shipments (i.e., more lumpiness) in international trade. This finding is pervasive across broad product categories, but most apparent for industrial supplies, parts and accessories, and food products.
papers.summary_en: International trade happens in lumps, not smoothly: a firm exports to a country, then pauses, then exports again. This paper shows why. Using detailed US and Spanish customs data, the authors show that when per-shipment costs are higher, shipments are larger but less frequent (more lumpy). The relationship holds across all broad product categories — but is strongest for industrial supplies, parts/accessories, and food products. A clean empirical grounding for an important but underappreciated source of trade costs: the fixed cost of each individual shipment.
papers.data_used: Administrative customs data from US and Spain: shipment-level records of exports with product, destination, weight, and value. Covers the full set of export shipments over multiple years.
papers.policy_relevance: Relevant to Hungarian trade-facilitation policy (customs, logistics, port infrastructure). The paper supports measures that reduce per-shipment costs (digital customs, border-crossing speed): their effect is to make trade less lumpy and more frequent, which improves buyer flexibility. Specifically relevant to EU customs harmonisation and to ITM infrastructure investments in transport corridors. Caveat: the evidence is from US and Spain; Hungary's role in EU supply chains differs, but the mechanism (per-shipment fixed costs) is general.

papers.title: Administrative barriers to trade
papers.abstract: We build a model of administrative barriers to trade to understand how they affect trade volumes, shipping decisions and welfare. Because administrative costs are incurred with every shipment, exporters have to decide how to break up total trade into individual shipments. Consumers value frequent shipments, because they enable them to consume close to their preferred dates. Hence per-shipment costs create a welfare loss. We derive a gravity equation in our model and show that administrative costs can be expressed as bilateral ad-valorem trade costs. We estimate the ad-valorem equivalent in Spanish shipment-level export data and find it to be large. A 50% reduction in per-shipment costs is equivalent to a 9 percentage point reduction in tariffs. Our model and estimates help explain why policy makers emphasize trade facilitation and why trade within customs unions is larger than trade within free trade areas.

papers.title: Winners and Losers of Globalization: Sixteen Challenges for Measurement and Theory
papers.abstract: The goal of this chapter is to summarize the state of the art in research in international trade and global production, and discuss issues relevant to European policymakers. Much of recent research on globalization is primarily empirical, owing to the proliferation of available data. We begin by discussing recent advances in measuring the causes and effects of globalization, and discussing the particular data challenges that have emerged. We then turn to theories of trade and global production, first summarizing the conclusions on which there is a broad consensus in the field. We discuss new insights that may be relevant for policy-makers, and open research questions.

papers.title: Estimation and inference for distribution and quantile functions in endogenous treatment effect models
papers.abstract: Given a standard endogenous treatment effect model, we propose nonparametric estimation and inference procedures for the distribution and quantile functions of the potential outcomes among compliers, as well as the local quantile treatment effect function. The preliminary distribution function estimator is a weighted average of indicator functions, but is not monotonically increasing in general. We therefore propose a simple monotonizing method for proper distribution function estimation, and obtain the quantile function estimator by inversion. Our monotonizing method is an alternative to Chernozhukov et al. (2010 Chernozhukov, V., Fernández-Val, I., Galichon, A. (2010). Quantile and probability curves without crossing. Econometrica 78(3):1093–1125.[Crossref], [Web of Science ®] , [Google Scholar]) and is arguably preferable when the outcome has unbounded support. We show that all the estimators converge weakly to Gaussian processes at the parametric rate, and propose a multiplier bootstrap for uniform inference. Our uniform results thus generalize the pointwise theory developed by Frölich and Melly (2013 Frölich, M., Melly, B. (2013). Unconditional quantile treatment effects under endogeneity. Journal of Business & Economic Statistics 31(3):346–357. doi:10.1080/07350015.2013.803869[Taylor & Francis Online], [Web of Science ®] , [Google Scholar]). Monte Carlo simulations and an application to the effect of fertility on family income distribution illustrate the use of the methods. All results extend to the subpopulation of treated compliers as well.
papers.summary_en: How do you estimate the distribution — not just the mean — of treatment effects when treatment is endogenous (think: voluntary program participation)? This methodological paper proposes nonparametric estimation of the distribution and quantile functions of potential outcomes among compliers. The approach uses a simple monotonisation method that works even when the outcome is unbounded, improving on the Chernozhukov–Fernández-Val–Galichon (2010) alternative. The paper develops the asymptotic theory and provides a multiplier-bootstrap procedure for proper confidence bands.
papers.summary_hu: Hogyan becsüljük a kezelési hatások teljes eloszlását — nem csak az átlagát —, amikor a kezelés endogén (például önkéntes programrészvétel)? A módszertani tanulmány nem-parametrikus becslést javasol a potenciális kimenetelek eloszlására és kvantilis-függvényeire az megfelelők körében. A megközelítés egy egyszerű monotonizáló módszert használ, amely akkor is működik, ha a kimenetel nem korlátos, javítva a Chernozhukov–Fernández-Val–Galichon (2010) alternatívát. A tanulmány kifejleszti az aszimptotikus elméletet és megfelelő konfidencia-sávokhoz multiplier-bootstrap eljárást ad.
papers.data_used: Methodological paper; no specific empirical application beyond simulation studies.
papers.data_used_hu: Módszertani tanulmány; nincs specifikus empirikus alkalmazás a szimulációs tanulmányokon túl.
papers.policy_relevance: For Hungarian policy evaluation: when a program's impact is not uniform across the population (e.g., some participants gain much more than average), knowing the whole distribution matters. This estimator lets evaluators properly quantify how much of the gain goes to which segment of program participants — useful for targeted policies, means-tested programs, and heterogeneous-response interventions.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: Magyar szakpolitikai értékeléshez: amikor egy program hatása nem egyenletes a népességben (pl. egyes résztvevők sokkal többet nyernek az átlagnál), az egész eloszlás ismerete számít. Ez a becslő lehetővé teszi az értékelőknek, hogy megfelelően kvantifikálják, a nyereség mennyi megy a program résztvevőinek mely szegmenséhez — hasznos a célzott politikáknak, rászorultsági alapú programoknak és heterogén-válaszú beavatkozásoknak.

papers.title: Counterfactual Treatment Effects: Estimation and Inference
papers.journal: Journal of Business and Economic Statistics
papers.abstract: This article proposes statistical methods to evaluate the quantile counterfactual treatment effect (QCTE) if one were to change the composition of the population targeted by a status quo program. QCTE enables a researcher to carry out an ex-ante assessment of the distributional impact of certain policy interventions or to investigate the possible explanations for treatment effect heterogeneity. Assuming unconfoundedness and invariance of the conditional distributions of the potential outcomes, QCTE is identified and can be nonparametrically estimated by a kernel-based method. Viewed as a random function over the continuum of quantile indices, the estimator converges weakly to a zero mean Gaussian process at the parametric rate. We propose a multiplier bootstrap procedure to construct uniform confidence bands, and provide similar results for average effects and for the counterfactually treated subpopulation. We also present Monte Carlo simulations and two counterfactual exercises that provide insight into the heterogeneous earnings effects of the Job Corps training program in the United States.
papers.summary_en: If we changed who gets a particular social program, what would its effects look like? This methodological paper develops estimation and inference for the 'quantile counterfactual treatment effect' — the distribution of outcomes that would result if the treated population were different. The approach handles both ex-ante policy simulations and analyses of treatment-effect heterogeneity. Monte Carlo simulations and two empirical counterfactuals illustrate the method.
papers.summary_hu: Ha megváltoztatnánk, ki kap egy bizonyos szociális programot, hogyan néznének ki a hatások? A módszertani tanulmány becslést és következtetést fejleszt a „kvantilis ellenpéldás kezelési hatásra" — ami azt adja meg, milyen kimenetel-eloszlás lenne, ha a kezelt népesség más lenne. A megközelítés mind az előzetes szakpolitikai szimulációt, mind a kezelési-hatás-heterogenitás elemzését kezeli. Monte Carlo-szimulációk és két empirikus ellenpélda illusztrálja a módszert.
papers.data_used: Methodological paper; two counterfactual exercises illustrate the approach but the main contribution is statistical.
papers.data_used_hu: Módszertani tanulmány; két ellenpéldás gyakorlat illusztrálja a megközelítést, de a fő hozzájárulás statisztikai.
papers.policy_relevance: Useful for Hungarian policy-simulation work: when a ministry wants to know 'what would happen if we expanded this program to group X?', this method provides proper counterfactual distributions with confidence bands — not just point estimates. Applies to means-tested benefit reforms, targeted subsidies, and training-program expansions.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: Hasznos magyar szakpolitikai-szimulációs munkához: amikor egy minisztérium tudni akarja, hogy „mi történne, ha kiterjesztenénk ezt a programot X csoportra?", ez a módszer megfelelő ellenpéldás eloszlásokat ad konfidencia-sávokkal — nem csak pontbecsléseket. Alkalmazható rászorultsági alapú juttatás-reformokhoz, célzott támogatásokhoz és képzési-program kiterjesztésekhez.

papers.title: How to enter high-opportunity places? The role of social contacts for residential mobility
papers.abstract: The aim of this article is to analyze the contribution of social ties to moving to high-opportunity locations and assess whether their effect is more pronounced for low-income individuals as a compensation for economic resources. This is done by utilizing Swedish administrative data and by focusing on a wide range of relationships (observed directly or inferred from the data): close and distant family ties, former co-workers and university peers. For estimating the effect of social ties, we use linear probability models, where observed migration is regressed on individual-specific and target-specific characteristics. To account for the nonrandom sorting of movers between locations, we apply sending municipality–target municipality–occupation fixed effects. Our results suggest that there is a positive relationship between migration and the presence of links at given targets for all the examined contact types. The effects are even stronger if the targets are hard-to-reach municipalities (located in Stockholm County or a municipality with higher housing prices). We also demonstrate that, when moving to such opportunity-rich areas, ties to former co-workers and university peers are even more essential assets for those with limited resources. Furthermore, we show that direct help with housing through contacts is an existing factor that contributes to the effect of social networks on residential mobility. The results reinforce the idea that social ties may be of great help in reducing barriers to mobility and can be used to compensate for limited economic resources. We demonstrate the validity of our fixed-effect estimation strategy using a placebo contact approach.

papers.title: Egyetemről a munkaerőpiacra. Felsőoktatási ismeretségek hatása a munkaerőpiaci kilátásokra
papers.abstract: Tanulmányunkban azt vizsgáljuk, hogy a mesterképzésben egy időben tanuló szaktársak milyen hatást gyakorolnak egymás későbbi munkaerőpiaci kilátásaira a végzettség megszerzését követő néhány évben. Az elemzést olyan összekötött államigazgatási adatokon végeztük, amelyek a 2003 és 2017 közötti időszakra tartalmazzák a magyarországi népesség 50 százalékának munkaerőpiaci és oktatási adatait. Becsléseink a 2010 és 2017 között mesterszakon végzett hallgatókat célozzák a korai munkakeresés és munkavállalás időszakában. A szaktársi ismeretségek hatását a közös egyetemi szakon egy félévben kezdő és ugyanazon félévben végző hallgatók munkahelyi találkozásával közelítjük. Eredményeink szerint a friss diplomások kimutathatóan nagyobb valószínűséggel nyernek felvételt olyan munkahelyekre, ahol egykori szaktársaik dolgoznak. Ez a hatás meglehetősen stabil, tehát azt elsősorban nem egyetemi szak- vagy általános értelemben vett szakspecifikus mechanizmusok vezérlik. Noha teljesen biztosak nem lehetünk a szaktársak segítő közreműködésében (legyen szó akár információátadásról, akár ajánlásról), alternatív becsléseink valószínűsítik ezt. Az egykori szaktársak vállalati jelenléte az újabban keletkezett munkaviszonyok jobb relatív minőségi jellemzőivel is párosul: magasabb kezdő bérrel, presztítízzsel és státussal, tartósabb munkaviszonnyal.* Journal of Economic Literature (JEL) kód: J24, J30, J62, J64, M51, Z13.

papers.title: University peers and career prospects: The impact of university ties on early labor market outcomes
papers.abstract: By using extensive Hungarian administrative data, this study aims to provide empirical evidence that former university ties strongly influence the labour market outcomes of individuals, even early in their careers. The estimates focus on the early career paths of graduates who obtained a master's degree between 2010 and 2017. As direct information on social contacts is not available in the dataset, we proxy university peers as students who started and finished the same university programmes (bachelor's or master's) in the same semester. Our results suggest that individuals are more likely to get hired by given firms if their former peers work there. The measured effects are considered significant and quite robust, even after controlling for the important sources of potential bias. Although we cannot present exact proof of the direct help of contacts, we provide suggestive evidence that seems to confirm the existence of such assistance. Our findings also revealed that the measured benefits are mainly attributable to connections from bachelor's studies. The effect of master's peers is mostly driven by the selection of individuals alongside prevalent study track-firm pathways. By comparing entries into new firms with and without peers, we also show that graduates with links have better labor market outcomes after hiring: they earn higher wages, obtain better and more prestigious positions, and stay longer at their new firm. The results draw attention to the importance of university peers in the labour market and contribute to the discussions about the determinants of early labour market success.

papers.title: An Open Economy DSGE Model with Search-and-Matching Frictions: The Case of Hungary
papers.abstract: This article builds and estimates a medium scale, small open economy DSGE model augmented with search-and-matching frictions in the labor market, and different wage setting behavior in new and existing jobs. The model is estimated using Hungarian data between 2001–2008. We find that: (i) the inclusion of matching frictions significantly improves the model’s empirical fit; (ii) the extent of new hires wage rigidity is quantitatively important for key macro variables; (iii) labor market shocks do not play an important role in inflation dynamics, but the structure of the labor market influences the monetary transmission mechanism.

papers.title: On Connected Strongly-Proportional Cake-Cutting
papers.journal: Frontiers in artificial intelligence and applications
papers.abstract: We investigate the problem of fairly dividing a divisible heterogeneous resource, also known as a cake, among a set of agents who may have different entitlements. We characterize the existence of a connected strongly-proportional allocation—one in which every agent receives a contiguous piece worth strictly more than their proportional share. The characterization is supplemented with an algorithm that determines its existence using O(n·2n) queries. We devise a simpler characterization for agents with strictly positive valuations and with equal entitlements, and present an algorithm to determine the existence of such an allocation using O(n2) queries. We provide matching lower bounds in the number of queries for both algorithms. When a connected strongly-proportional allocation exists, we show that it can also be computed using a similar number of queries. The full version is available at https://arxiv.org/abs/2312.15326.

papers.title: Bin Packing with Fixed Number of Bins Revisited
Indirect Effects of Access to Finance
papers.abstract: We created experimental variation across markets in China in the share of firms having access to a new loan product. Access to finance had a large positive direct effect on the performance of treated firms but a similar-sized negative indirect effect on that of firms with treated competitors, leading to nondetectable gains in producer surplus. Access to finance had a positive direct effect on business quality and consumer satisfaction and a negative effect on price, which were not offset by indirect effects, implying net gains in consumer surplus. We document other indirect effects and combine effects in a welfare evaluation. (JEL D22, G21, G32, L81, O16, P31, P34)
papers.summary_en: When small firms get better access to finance, they grow. But their competitors — who didn't get the new loans — may lose business. This paper ran a randomised experiment in Chinese retail markets, varying across cities how many firms were offered a new loan product. Treated firms performed much better; their untreated competitors performed correspondingly worse. Net producer-surplus gain: essentially zero. But consumers gained: treated firms delivered better product quality and satisfaction without raising prices as much as their untreated competitors, producing real welfare gains. The paper is a model of how to evaluate finance programmes while accounting for spillover effects.
papers.summary_hu: Amikor a kis cégek jobb hozzáférést kapnak a pénzügyi forrásokhoz, nőnek. De a versenytársaik — akik nem kaptak új hiteleket — veszíthetnek üzletet. A tanulmány randomizált kísérletet futott kínai kiskereskedelmi piacokon, városok között változtatva, hogy a cégek hány százalékának kínáltak egy új hiteltermékét. A kezelt cégek sokkal jobban teljesítettek; a kezeletlen versenytársaik arányosan rosszabbul. Nettó termelői többletnyereség: lényegében nulla. De a fogyasztók nyertek: a kezelt cégek jobb termékminőséget és ügyfél-elégedettséget nyújtottak anélkül, hogy az árakat annyira emelték volna, mint a kezeletlen versenytársak, valós jóléti nyereséget termelve. A tanulmány mintamód a pénzügyi programok értékelésére az átterjedési hatások figyelembevételével.
papers.data_used: Randomised market-level experiment in China involving ~1,000 small firms across multiple cities, 2018–2021; firm performance tracked via sales data, mystery shoppers, and customer surveys.
papers.data_used_hu: Randomizált piac-szintű kísérlet Kínában, ~1 000 kis céget érintve több városban, 2018–2021; cégteljesítmény nyomon követve eladási adatokon, rejtett vásárlókon és ügyfél-felméréseken keresztül.
papers.policy_relevance: For Hungarian SME-finance policy (MNB, MFB, Ministry of Economy): the paper's core lesson — that firm-level evaluation of finance programmes understates displacement effects on competitors — applies directly to Hungarian SME loan programmes. A Hungarian programme showing positive treated-firm effects may be net-neutral to the SME sector but still beneficial to consumers. Argues for market-level (not just firm-level) evaluation design. Chinese retail setting; the mechanism applies wherever financial constraints are binding on firms.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: A magyar kkv-finanszírozási politikához (MNB, MFB, Gazdasági Minisztérium): a tanulmány fő leckéje — hogy a pénzügyi programok cégszintű értékelése alábecsüli a versenytársakra gyakorolt elmozdító hatásokat — közvetlenül vonatkozik a magyar kkv-hitelprogramokra. Egy magyar program, amely pozitív kezelt-cég hatást mutat, lehet nettó-semleges a kkv-szektornak, de még mindig hasznos a fogyasztóknak. Érvel a piac-szintű (nem csak cégszintű) értékelés-tervezés mellett. Kínai kiskereskedelmi környezet; a mechanizmus mindenhol alkalmazható, ahol a pénzügyi korlátok megkötik a cégeket.

papers.title: Deceptive Features on Platforms
papers.abstract: Abstract Many products sold on online platforms have additional features like fees for services, shipping, luggage, upgrades, and so on. We study when a two-sided platform shrouds additional features towards potentially naive buyers. We explore a novel mechanism according to which platforms shroud to manipulate network externalities between buyers and sellers. Exploring this mechanism, we argue the advent of online marketplaces led to less transparent markets. First, platforms have stronger incentives to shroud seller fees than sellers themselves. Second, platforms shroud their own fees less if they earn more revenue from sellers; so when sellers on the platform compete more fiercely, platforms—somewhat perversely—shroud more. We connect these results to many applications and the current debate on regulating online platforms.
papers.summary_en: Why do online platforms bury extra fees (shipping, service charges, luggage) in fine print? This paper develops a model where shrouding these charges is a strategic choice by the platform — not just sellers — because it manipulates network externalities between buyers and sellers. Two counter-intuitive results emerge: platforms shroud their own fees less when they earn more revenue from sellers, and platforms shroud sellers' fees even when sellers themselves would prefer transparency. The advent of online marketplaces has made markets less transparent. The paper gives a framework for thinking about platform-regulation rules like the EU Digital Markets Act.
papers.summary_hu: Miért temetik az online platformok az extra díjakat (szállítás, szolgáltatás, poggyász) kisbetűs részekbe? A tanulmány modellt fejleszt, amelyben a díjak elrejtése a platform — nem csak az eladók — stratégiai döntése, mert manipulálja a vásárlók és eladók közötti hálózati externáliákat. Két ellenintuitív eredmény: a platformok kisebb mértékben rejtik saját díjaikat, amikor több bevételt kapnak eladóktól, és a platformok elrejtik az eladók díjait akkor is, amikor maguk az eladók inkább átláthatóságot szeretnének. Az online piacterek megjelenése átláthatatlanabbá tette a piacokat. A tanulmány keretet ad az olyan platform-szabályozási szabályok gondolkodáshoz, mint az EU Digitális Piacok Tarvezetéről szóló törvény (DMA).
papers.data_used: Theoretical paper; no empirical data. The authors reference examples from Amazon, Booking.com, Uber, and Apple's App Store to motivate assumptions.
papers.data_used_hu: Elméleti tanulmány; nincs empirikus adat. A szerzők Amazon, Booking.com, Uber és Apple App Store példákra hivatkoznak a feltevések motiválásához.
papers.policy_relevance: Directly relevant to Hungarian competition policy (GVH) and EU Digital Markets Act enforcement. Regulatory implications: (1) Mandatory "all-in" pricing on platforms is a stronger lever than transparency encouragement; (2) Platforms themselves (not just sellers on them) are a legitimate target of fee-disclosure rules; (3) Revenue structure between platform and seller matters for shrouding incentives. Theoretical; specific DMA enforcement decisions need empirical follow-up.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: Közvetlenül releváns a magyar versenypolitikához (GVH) és az EU Digitális Piacok Tarvezetéről szóló törvény (DMA) kikényszerítéséhez. Szabályozási következmények: (1) A kötelező „minden díjat feltüntető" árazás a platformokon erősebb eszköz, mint az átláthatóság ösztönzése; (2) Maguk a platformok (nem csak az eladók rajtuk) legitim cél a díj-közzétételi szabályokhoz; (3) A platform és az eladó közötti bevétel-struktúra számít az elrejtés-ösztönzőknek. Elméleti; konkrét DMA-kikényszerítési döntések empirikus követést igényelnek.

papers.title: On the Market for Venture Capital
papers.abstract: We propose a theory of the market for venture capital that links the excess return to venture equity to the scarcity of venture capitalists (VCs). High returns make the VCs more selective and eager to terminate nonperforming ventures because they can move on to new ones. The scarcity of VCs enables them to internalize their social value, and the competitive equilibrium is socially optimal. Moreover, the bilaterally efficient contract is a simple equity contract. We estimate the model for the period 1989–2001 and compute the excess return to venture capital, which turns out to be 8.6 percent. Finally, we back out the return of solo entrepreneurs, which is increasing in their wealth and ranges between zero and 3.5 percent.
papers.summary_en: Venture capital (VC) firms consistently earn excess returns. Is that due to exceptional skill? This paper argues: no, it's because venture capitalists are scarce. In equilibrium, high returns make VCs more selective and more willing to end underperforming ventures (since they can move on to new ones). The scarcity lets VCs internalise their own social value, and the competitive equilibrium is socially efficient. Simple equity contracts emerge as the bilaterally-efficient arrangement. The authors estimate the model on 1989–2001 data and find excess VC returns of 8.6%, with solo-entrepreneur returns ranging from zero to 3.5% depending on wealth.
papers.data_used: Quantitative model estimated on US VC and startup data 1989–2001. Returns data on venture-backed companies and solo entrepreneurs.
papers.policy_relevance: Relevant to Hungary's startup-support ecosystem (Design Terminal, Hiventures, EU structural funds for VC). Implication: crowd-in public VC can raise entrepreneurial activity but will tend to lower returns; scarcity of skilled VC is a binding constraint, and capital alone is insufficient without the screening and monitoring that VCs provide. Relevant to evaluation of the MFB/state-backed VC programmes. Caveat: the model is calibrated to US data; Hungarian VC-market depth and the role of state intervention are different.

papers.title: Temporary Protection and Technology Adoption: Evidence from the Napoleonic Blockade
papers.abstract: This paper asks whether temporary trade protection can help countries develop new industries. Using Napoleon's blockade of British trade as a natural experiment, I show that French regions whose exposure to British imports was more reduced by the blockade saw faster adoption of mechanised cotton spinning and retained this advantage for decades after the blockade ended.
papers.summary_en: Can a country build a new industry by temporarily shielding it from foreign competition? Using Napoleon's 1806–1813 blockade of British trade — which cut off imports unevenly across French regions — this paper shows that more-protected regions adopted mechanised cotton spinning faster, and kept the advantage long after the blockade was lifted. The paper is a rare piece of credible empirical evidence for the classical infant-industry argument behind industrial policy.
papers.summary_hu: Egy ország kiépíthet-e egy új iparágat, ha időlegesen megvédi a külföldi versenytől? Napoleon 1806–1813-as brit-kereskedelmi blokádját használva — amely egyenetlenül vágta el a francia régiókat az importoktól —, a tanulmány megmutatja, hogy a jobban védett régiók gyorsabban adoptálták a gépesített gyapot-fonást, és megtartották az előnyt jóval a blokád feloldása után. A tanulmány ritka, hiteles empirikus bizonyíték a klasszikus „kezdő-ipar" érvre, amely az iparpolitika mögött áll.
papers.data_used: Historical French administrative data on textile mills and spindles by département, 1803–1840, combined with measures of each region's effective exposure to the British blockade based on distance from the North Sea coast and shipping routes.
papers.data_used_hu: Történelmi francia adminisztratív adatok textil-gyárakra és orsókra départementenként 1803–1840 között, kombinálva a brit blokádnak való tényleges kitettség mérőszámaival, amelyek az Északi-tengerből való távolságra és hajózási útvonalakra épülnek.
papers.policy_relevance: Offers one of the cleanest empirical cases for the idea that time-limited trade protection can seed a lasting industry. Caveats: the setting is early-19th-century textiles and a very large trade shock; generalising to modern industrial policy requires care about which industries and shock sizes are comparable. Relevant for: Hungarian Ministry of Economy officials designing targeted industrial policy under EU state-aid rules.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: Az egyik legtisztább empirikus eset arra, hogy időleges kereskedelmi védelem tartós iparhoz vezethet. Figyelmeztetés: a környezet 19. század eleji textil és nagyon nagy kereskedelmi sokk; a modern iparpolitikára való általánosítás óvatosságot igényel, mely iparágakban és sokknagyságokban lehet összehasonlítható. Releváns a magyar Gazdasági Minisztérium tisztviselőinek, akik célzott iparpolitikát terveznek az EU állami-támogatási szabályok alatt.

popular_links.title: Temporary protection and technology adoption: Evidence from the Napoleonic blockade

papers.title: Spinoffs and tie formation in cluster knowledge networks
papers.abstract: Abstract It is generally acknowledged that in order to have access to locally accumulated industrial knowledge, firms have to collaborate and take part in cluster knowledge networks. This study argues that the inherited capabilities of spinoff enable them to cooperate and exchange knowledge more easily and to gain more from positive knowledge externalities in clusters. The basis of the analysis is a relational dataset on a printing and paper product cluster in Hungary, and I use exponential random graph models to explain the formation of knowledge ties. I demonstrate that besides geographical proximity, ownership similarity and network structural effects, being a spinoff company enhances tie formation in the local network. Results suggest that spinoffs are indeed more likely to collaborate and take advantage of knowledge concentration.

papers.title: A tudáshálózatok időbeli változásának vizsgálati lehetőségei
papers.abstract: Kikkel alakítanak ki kapcsolatot a gazdaság szereplői? Milyen jellemzők befolyásolják e kapcsolatok alakulását? Örökké tartanak ezek az együttműködések? A hálózatok dinamikájának vizsgálatával az utóbbi időben többen tettek kísérletet ezeknek a kérdéseknek a megválaszolására. Jelen tanulmánnyal az a célunk, hogy felhívjuk a hazai közgazdászközösség figyelmét a hálózatok dinamikus megközelítésének fontosságára és hazai kutatási perspektíváira. Szakirodalmi áttekintésünkben a regionális klasztereket és iparágakat átfogó tudáshálózatok változását magyarázó regionális gazdaságtani tanulmányokra helyezzük a hangsúlyt, mivel az empirikus módszerek alkalmazása jelenleg ezen megközelítések esetében a leggazdagabb. Összegző megállapításunk, hogy a regionális klaszterek tudáshálózataiban a beágya zó dottság és a kohézió, míg az iparági tudáshálózatokban az ágazati tapasztalat, a földrajzi közelség és a közös partnerek megléte segítik az együttműködések kialakulását. A dinamikus hálózatelemzés eszközeinek és eredményeinek adaptálása és továbbfejlesztése érdemben hozzájárulhat a gazdasági szereplők közötti kapcsolatrendszerek megértéséhez.* Journal of Economic Literature (JEL) kód: D85, L14, R11, O31.

papers.title: Co-location of skill-related suppliers: advancing coagglomeration research using firm-to-firm network data
papers.abstract: Abstract Firms in industry clusters benefit from locating close to suppliers and customers. However, the pervasiveness of global value chains questions the need for co-location in buyer–supplier relationships. We propose that supply-chain partners are more likely to co-locate if they exchange not only goods but also know-how, implying superadditivity of Marshallian agglomeration channels. We test this in a coagglomeration framework using microdata for Hungary—a small, open economy deeply embedded in global value chains—examining co-location, labor flows, and value chains between firms and industries. We find that supply chains foster co-location primarily among firms in skill-related industries.
papers.summary_en: Why do firms in industry clusters benefit from being near their suppliers and customers? The textbook answer points to goods flows. This paper argues it's also about know-how flows. If supply-chain partners exchange not just inputs but tacit knowledge, their benefits from co-locating should be larger than Marshallian theory suggests. Using Hungarian microdata on co-location, labour flows, and firm-to-firm value chains, the authors test the prediction. Finding: supply chains foster co-location primarily among firms in skill-related industries — confirming the know-how channel. A cleaner empirical framework for thinking about industrial-cluster policy.
papers.data_used: Hungarian administrative microdata on firms: inter-firm value-chain relationships, labour mobility between firms, firm-level co-location. Covers firms across all industries in Hungary.
papers.policy_relevance: Directly relevant to Hungarian regional-development and industrial-policy design (ITM, local governments). The paper supports cluster-support policies that prioritize skill-related industries and recognise know-how transfer as a distinct benefit of co-location. Relevant to EU cohesion-fund targeting and to Hungarian special-economic-zone policy. Caveat: the data are recent and Hungarian; the skill-relatedness channel's strength in other settings may vary.

papers.title: Creation and persistence of ties in cluster knowledge networks
papers.abstract: Knowledge networks are important to understand learning in industry clusters but surprisingly little is known about what drives the formation, persistence and dissolution of ties. Applying stochastic actor-oriented models on longitudinal relational data from a mature cluster in a medium-tech industry, we show that triadic closure and geographical proximity increase the probability of tie creation but does not influence tie persistence. Cognitive proximity is positively correlated to tie persistence but firms create ties to cognitively proximate firms only if they are loosely connected through common third partners. We propose a micro-perspective to understand how endogenous network effects, cognitive proximity of actors and their interplay influence the evolutionary process of network formation in clusters.

papers.title: Technology Adoption and Productivity Growth: Evidence from Industrialization in France
papers.abstract: New technologies tend to be adopted slowly and – even after being adopted – take time to be reflected in higher aggregate productivity. One prominent explanation for these patterns is the need to reorganize production, which often goes hand-in-hand with major technological breakthroughs. We study a unique setting that allows us to examine the empirical relevance of this explanation: the adoption of mechanized cotton spinning during the First Industrial Revolution in France. The new technology required reorganizing production by moving workers from their homes to the newly-formed factories. Using a novel hand-collected plant-level dataset from French archival sources, we show that productivity growth in mechanized cotton spinning was driven by the disappearance of plants in the lower tail – in contrast to other sectors that did not need to reorganize when new technologies were introduced. We provide evidence that this was driven by organizational challenges such as developing optimal plant layout. A process of ‘trial and error’ led to initially low and widely dispersed productivity, and – in the subsequent decades – to high productivity growth as knowledge diffused through the economy and new entrants adopted improved methods of organizing production.
papers.summary_en: New technologies often take years to show up in aggregate productivity. Why? This paper shows that realising the gains usually requires reorganising production — a slow, costly transition. The empirical setting is the First Industrial Revolution in France: mechanised cotton spinning required moving workers out of their homes into the newly-formed factories. With a hand-collected plant-level dataset from French archives, the authors show that in cotton spinning, productivity growth came from the exit of low-productivity plants rather than improvements within plants. In other sectors where reorganisation was not needed, the pattern is different. Takeaway: technological breakthroughs and organisational change are inseparable.
papers.data_used: Hand-collected plant-level historical data from French archival sources during the First Industrial Revolution (early 1800s). Covers mechanised cotton spinning plus comparison sectors that did not require organisational reconfiguration. Plant-level outputs, inputs, and entry/exit.
papers.policy_relevance: Directly relevant to Hungary's productivity-catch-up strategy and AI/digitalisation policy. The paper is a warning to expect quick productivity payoffs from General Purpose Technologies (AI, Industry 4.0): the gains show up as laggard firms exit, not as broad-based improvement, and the transition takes time. For the Ministry of Economic Development, SzéChenyi programmes, and EU funds earmarked for digitalisation, this implies patience in evaluation, complementary investment in management practices and reallocation support, and not expecting technology subsidies alone to raise measured productivity quickly. Caveat: the historical French case speaks to the mechanism, not to precise Hungarian magnitudes.

papers.title: Industrial Policy and the Great Divergence
papers.abstract: We discuss recent work evaluating the role of the government in shaping the economy during the long nineteenth century, a practice we refer to as industrial policy. States deployed a vast variety of different policies aimed primarily, but not exclusively, at fostering industrialization. A thin but growing literature has started to evaluate the economic effects of these policies, but many questions remain open for study.
papers.summary_en: Industrial policy is experiencing a revival in global debate. This Annual Review piece synthesises what economic historians and applied economists have learned about industrial policy in the long 19th century, when states deployed tariffs, subsidies, export bounties, and infrastructure to try to industrialise. The authors argue that a growing, still-thin literature shows selective evidence for industrial-policy success when policy, institutions, and timing align — but that much of the older historical literature is methodologically weak. The review sets an agenda for future work.
papers.summary_hu: Az iparpolitika reneszánszát éli a globális vitában. Ez az Annual Review-darab összefoglalja, amit a gazdaságtörténészek és alkalmazott közgazdászok megtanultak a hosszú 19. század iparpolitikájáról, amikor az államok vámokat, támogatásokat, export-prémiumokat és infrastruktúrát vetettek be, hogy iparosodjanak. A szerzők érvelnek, hogy egy növekvő, de még vékony irodalom válogatott bizonyítékot mutat az iparpolitika sikeressége mellett, amikor a politika, az intézmények és az időzítés összhangban vannak — de a korábbi történelmi irodalom nagy része módszertanilag gyenge. Az áttekintés agendát állít a jövőbeli munka számára.
papers.data_used: Review paper synthesising ~40 published studies of 19th-century industrial policy; no new data.
papers.data_used_hu: Áttekintő tanulmány, amely ~40 publikált tanulmányt szintetizál a 19. századi iparpolitikáról; nincs új adat.
papers.policy_relevance: Framework reading for Hungarian industrial-policy debates (Ministry of Economy): supports the view that well-targeted industrial policy can work, but with strong caveats on complementary institutions (effective state capacity, education systems, trade policy alignment). Useful as a sobering antidote to casual claims that "industrial policy worked in Korea, so it will work in Hungary". Review format: individual policy prescriptions require reading the underlying papers.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: Keret-olvasmány a magyar iparpolitikai vitákhoz (Gazdasági Minisztérium): támogatja azt a nézetet, hogy a jól célzott iparpolitika működhet, de erős kikötésekkel a kiegészítő intézményekre (hatékony állami kapacitás, oktatási rendszerek, kereskedelempolitikai összhang). Hasznos mint józan ellenszer a „az iparpolitika működött Koreában, tehát Magyarországon is működni fog" típusú felületes állításokhoz. Áttekintő formátum: konkrét szakpolitikai előírások az alapul szolgáló tanulmányok olvasását igénylik.

papers.title: The software complexity of nations
papers.abstract: Despite the growing importance of the digital sector, research on economic complexity and its implications continues to rely mostly on administrative records—e.g. data on exports, patents, and employment—that have blind spots when it comes to the digital economy. In this paper we use data on the geography of programming languages used in open-source software to extend economic complexity ideas to the digital economy. We estimate a country's software economic complexity index (ECI software ) and show that it complements the ability of measures of complexity based on trade, patents, and research to account for international differences in GDP per capita, income inequality, and emissions. We also show that open-source software follows the principle of relatedness, meaning that a country's entries and exits in programming languages are partly explained by its current pattern of specialization. Together, these findings help extend economic complexity ideas and their policy implications to the digital economy. • We measure countries' software economic complexity using GitHub data • Software economic complexity predicts GDP, income inequality and emissions • We show that countries diversify into related programming languages over time • We extend economic complexity methods and their policy implications to the digital sector
papers.summary_en: "Economic complexity" measures — how sophisticated a country's production mix is — have been built from exports, patents, and employment, but those sources miss the digital economy entirely. This paper extends the framework to open-source software: which programming languages a country uses, and how rare-yet-productive those languages are. The resulting Software Economic Complexity Index predicts GDP per capita, income inequality, and emissions above and beyond trade-based measures. The paper shows that software capabilities, like physical-goods capabilities, follow a "principle of relatedness": countries move into languages related to what they already know.
papers.summary_hu: A „gazdasági komplexitás" mérőszámok — mennyire szofisztikált egy ország termelési keveréke — exportokból, szabadalmakból és foglalkoztatásból épültek, de ezek a források teljesen kihagyják a digitális gazdaságot. A tanulmány a keretet nyílt forráskódú szoftverre bővíti: mely programozási nyelveket használ egy ország, és mennyire ritkák-de-produktívak ezek a nyelvek. Az eredményül kapott Szoftver Gazdasági Komplexitási Index előrejelzi a GDP/fő, jövedelmi egyenlőtlenség és emissziók kérdéseit a kereskedelem-alapú mérőszámokon túl. A tanulmány megmutatja, hogy a szoftver-képességek, mint a fizikai-árukhoz kötődő képességek, „kapcsolódási elvet" követnek: az országok olyan nyelvekre lépnek át, amelyek kapcsolódnak ahhoz, amit már ismernek.
papers.data_used: Global GitHub activity data: country-level, programming-language-level panel of open-source-software contributions, ~2008–2023; matched to country-level macro data on GDP, inequality (Gini), and emissions.
papers.data_used_hu: Globális GitHub-tevékenységi adat: ország-szintű, programozási-nyelv-szintű panel nyílt forráskódú szoftver-hozzájárulásokról, ~2008–2023; ország-szintű makroadatokhoz illesztve a GDP-re, egyenlőtlenségre (Gini) és emissziókra.
papers.policy_relevance: For Hungarian digital-strategy policy (Ministry for Digital Development, NKFIH): measures a country's digital-sector readiness beyond narrow ICT-employment metrics. Relevant for deciding which programming-language ecosystems and open-source communities to support with public-sector talent-development programmes. Software specialisation, like physical-sector specialisation, exhibits path-dependence — Hungary is better positioned to advance in languages related to what Hungarian developers already contribute to.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: A magyar digitális-stratégiai politikához (Digitális Fejlesztési Minisztérium, NKFIH): egy ország digitális-szektor-készségét méri a szűk IKT-foglalkoztatási mérőszámokon túl. Releváns ahhoz, hogy eldöntsük, melyik programozási-nyelv-ökoszisztémákat és nyílt-forrás-közösségeket támogassuk közszféra-tehetségfejlesztő programokkal. A szoftver-specializáció, mint a fizikai-szektor-specializáció, útfüggést mutat — Magyarország jobban helyezkedik el olyan nyelvekben, amelyek kapcsolódnak a magyar fejlesztők meglévő hozzájárulásaihoz.

papers.title: Trade Liberalization and Gender Inequality
papers.abstract: We consider a model where firms differ in their productivity and workers are differentiated by skill and gender. A reduction in tariffs induces more productive firms to modernize their technology and enter the export market. New technologies involve computerized production processes and lower the need for physically demanding skills. As a result, the relative wage and employment of women improves in blue-collar tasks, but not in white-collar tasks. We empirically confirm these theoretical predictions using a panel of Mexican establishments and the tariff reductions associated with the North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA).
papers.summary_en: When a country cuts its import tariffs, who benefits — men or women? This paper builds and tests a theory where tariff cuts push productive firms to modernise their technology, which reduces the demand for physically demanding skills. The prediction: women's relative wages and employment rise in blue-collar jobs (where physical strength mattered most) but not in white-collar jobs. The authors confirm this using Mexican manufacturing data and NAFTA tariff cuts. Trade liberalisation can narrow gender gaps, but only on the factory floor, not in offices.
papers.summary_hu: Ha egy ország csökkenti importvámjait, ki nyer — férfiak vagy nők? A tanulmány egy elméletet épít és tesztel, amelyben a vámcsökkentések a termelékenyebb cégeket rávezetik a technológiájuk modernizálására, ami csökkenti a fizikailag megerőltető készségek iránti keresletet. Előrejelzés: a nők relatív bére és foglalkoztatása emelkedik a fizikai (blue-collar) munkákban (ahol a fizikai erő a leginkább számított), de nem a fehérgalléros munkákban. A szerzők megerősítik ezt mexikói gyártási adatokkal és a NAFTA vámcsökkentéseivel. A kereskedelmi liberalizáció szűkítheti a nemek közötti szakadékot, de csak a gyár padlóján, nem az irodákban.
