We quantitatively assess the model's predictions and find that it can generate patterns of volatility and development consistent with the data. (JEL D21, D24, E23, O33, O47)
papers.summary_en: Poor countries have volatile growth; rich countries grow smoothly. Why? This paper proposes that it is not a by-product of bad institutions but a mechanical consequence of development. Firms in early-development economies produce with a small number of specific, imperfectly-correlated input varieties, so they are exposed to variety-specific shocks. As the economy develops, firms adopt more varieties; average productivity rises (positive growth effect) and risk is diversified (falling volatility). The model quantitatively reproduces the observed volatility-income relationship across countries.
papers.summary_hu: A szegény országoknak volatilis növekedésük van; a gazdag országok egyenletesen nőnek. Miért? A tanulmány azt javasolja, hogy ez nem a rossz intézmények mellékterméke, hanem a fejlődés mechanikus következménye. A korai-fejlődési gazdaságokban a cégek kevés, specifikus, tökéletlenül korrelált input-fajtával termelnek, így fajta-specifikus sokkoknak vannak kitéve. Ahogy a gazdaság fejlődik, a cégek több fajtát adoptálnak; az átlagos termelékenység nő (pozitív növekedési hatás), és a kockázat diverzifikált (csökkenő volatilitás). A modell kvantitatívan reprodukálja a megfigyelt volatilitás-jövedelem viszonyt az országok között.
papers.data_used: Theoretical model calibrated against cross-country macro panel data on aggregate GDP volatility and per-capita income for ~90 countries, 1960–2007; from Penn World Tables and WDI.
papers.data_used_hu: Elméleti modell, ~90 ország GDP-volatilitási és egy főre eső jövedelmi adatára kalibrálva, 1960–2007; Penn World Tables és WDI.
papers.policy_relevance: For Hungarian macro-policy framing: aggregate volatility declines as the production structure diversifies, not just as institutions strengthen. Matters for industrial policy — supporting diversification across input varieties (firm-product-source combinations) has second-order benefits through volatility, beyond first-order productivity gains. Relevant to Ministry of Economy thinking on supply-chain resilience and energy-mix diversification. Model-based cross-country evidence; Hungary-specific calibration would make it operational.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: A magyar makró-politika keretezéséhez: az aggregált volatilitás csökken, ahogy a termelési szerkezet diverzifikálódik, nem csak ahogy az intézmények erősödnek. Számít az iparpolitikának — az input-fajták közötti diverzifikáció támogatása (cég-termék-forrás kombinációk) másodrendű előnyöket ad a volatilitáson át, az elsőrendű termelékenységi nyereségen túl. Releváns a Gazdasági Minisztérium ellátási-lánc rezilienciával és energiamix-diverzifikációval kapcsolatos gondolkodásához. Modell-alapú keresztország-bizonyíték; magyar-specifikus kalibráció operatívvá tenné.

papers.title: Resource-Constrained versus Demand-Constrained Systems
papers.abstract: Your use of the JSTOR archive indicates your acceptance of JSTOR&apos;s Terms and Conditions of Use, available at
papers.summary_en: Kornai's classic distinction: capitalist economies are demand-constrained (firms are willing to produce more but customers are not willing to buy), while socialist economies are resource-constrained (customers want more than firms can produce given available inputs). This Econometrica paper formalises the concept with a short, rigorous theoretical piece. The distinction became foundational for understanding the chronic shortages of socialist planning and — after 1989 — the massive shift in firm incentives once budget constraints hardened and prices freed. One of the most influential conceptual contributions to comparative economics of the 20th century.
papers.summary_hu: Kornai klasszikus megkülönböztetése: a kapitalista gazdaságok kereslet-korlátosak (a cégek hajlandók lennének többet termelni, de a vevők nem hajlandók többet venni), míg a szocialista gazdaságok erőforrás-korlátosak (a vevők többet akarnak, mint amennyit a cégek az elérhető inputokkal meg tudnak termelni). Ez az Econometrica-cikk rövid, szigorú elméleti darabként formalizálja a fogalmat. A megkülönböztetés alapvetővé vált a szocialista tervezés krónikus hiányainak megértésében, és — 1989 után — a cég-ösztönzők masszív eltolódásának, miután a költségvetési korlátok megkeményedtek és az árak felszabadultak. A 20. század egyik legbefolyásosabb elméleti hozzájárulása az összehasonlító közgazdaságtanhoz.
papers.data_used: Theoretical paper; illustrated with stylised facts from socialist economies of the 1970s.
papers.data_used_hu: Elméleti tanulmány; illusztrálva az 1970-es évek szocialista gazdaságainak stilizált tényeivel.
papers.policy_relevance: Historical-conceptual reference rather than immediate policy guide. For Hungarian policymakers, its enduring relevance is as a diagnostic language: where markets are "resource-constrained" (e.g., healthcare queues, social housing waitlists) the pathology looks like socialism's — and the solution is not more demand-side stimulus but relaxing the supply constraint. Useful framing in public-service debates. For more on policy implications, see Kornai's 1986 follow-up on soft budget constraints.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: Történelmi-fogalmi referencia, nem azonnali szakpolitikai útmutató. A magyar szakpolitikusok számára a tartós relevanciája diagnosztikai nyelvként érvényesül: ahol a piacok „erőforrás-korlátosak" (pl. egészségügyi várólisták, szociális lakás várólisták), a patológia úgy néz ki, mint a szocializmus — és a megoldás nem több kereslet-oldali ösztönzés, hanem a kínálati korlát lazítása. Hasznos keret közszolgálati vitákban. A szakpolitikai következményekhez lásd Kornai 1986-os follow-up-ját a puha költségvetési korlátokról.

papers.title: What the Change of System from Socialism to Capitalism Does and Does Not Mean
papers.abstract: This paper reflects on the first decade of post-socialist transition and argues that the change of system is a package: private ownership, market coordination, and the associated political institutions are mutually reinforcing. Partial reforms that retain key features of the old system — soft budget constraints in particular — produce poor outcomes, as the experience of several transition countries shows.
papers.summary_en: A retrospective on the first decade of post-socialist transition. Kornai argues that the move from plan to market is a package deal: private property, hard budget constraints, competition, and democratic institutions reinforce each other, and countries that tried to keep some features of the old system — especially soft budget constraints for state firms — got worse outcomes than those that pushed through the full transition. The paper is a canonical statement of the 'system paradigm' for understanding post-socialist economies.
papers.summary_hu: Visszatekintés a posztszocialista átmenet első évtizedére. Kornai érvel, hogy a tervezésről a piacra való áttérés csomagügylet: a magántulajdon, a kemény költségvetési korlátok, a verseny és a demokratikus intézmények egymást erősítik, és azok az országok, amelyek megpróbálták megtartani a régi rendszer néhány jellemzőjét — különösen a puha költségvetési korlátokat állami cégekre —, rosszabb kimeneteleket kaptak, mint azok, amelyek átvitték a teljes átmenetet. A tanulmány a „rendszer-paradigma" kanonikus megfogalmazása a posztszocialista gazdaságok megértéséhez.
papers.data_used: Narrative and comparative evidence from Central and Eastern European transition countries during the 1990s; no new micro data.
papers.data_used_hu: Narratív és összehasonlító bizonyíték közép- és kelet-európai átmenet-országokból az 1990-es években; nincs új mikroadat.
papers.policy_relevance: Foundational reading for anyone designing Hungarian economic policy in a CEE comparative frame. The paper's core warning — partial or selective reforms that preserve soft budget constraints do lasting damage — remains relevant for SOE policy, bank-resolution frameworks, and decisions about selectively bailing out politically connected firms. More conceptual than operational: use to frame arguments, not to estimate effects.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: Alapvető olvasmány mindenkinek, aki magyar gazdaságpolitikát tervez KKE-összehasonlító keretben. A tanulmány központi figyelmeztetése — a részleges vagy szelektív reformok, amelyek megőrzik a puha költségvetési korlátokat, tartós kárt okoznak — továbbra is releváns az SOE-politikához, a bank-rendezési keretekhez és a politikailag kapcsolt cégek szelektív kimentéséről szóló döntésekhez. Inkább fogalmi, mint operatív: használd érvek kereteként, ne hatásbecslésekhez.

papers.title: Health anxiety and patient behavior
EGO UTILITY, OVERCONFIDENCE, AND TASK CHOICE
papers.journal: Journal of the European Economic Association
papers.abstract: This paper models behavior when a decision maker cares about and manages her self-image. In addition to having preferences over material outcomes, the agent derives “ego utility ” from positive views about her ability to do well in a skill-sensitive, “ambitious, ” task. Although she uses Bayes ’ rule to update beliefs, she tends to become overconfident regarding which task is appropriate for her. If tasks are equally informative about ability, her task choice is also overconfident. If the ambitious task is more informative about ability, she might initially display underconfidence in behavior, and, if she is disappointed by her performance, later become too ambitious. People with ego utility prefer to acquire free information in smaller pieces. Applications to employee motivation and other economic settings are discussed. (JEL: D83, D11) 1.

papers.title: Emotional Agency
papers.abstract: This paper models interactions between a party with anticipatory emotions and a party who responds strategically to those emotions, a situation that is common in many health, political, employment, and personal settings. An "agent" has information with both decision-making value and emotional implications for an uninformed "principal" whose utility she wants to maximize. If she cannot directly reveal her information, to increase the principal's anticipatory utility she distorts instrumental decisions toward the action associated with good news. But because anticipatory utility derives from beliefs about instrumental outcomes, undistorted actions would yield higher ex ante total and anticipatory utility. If the agent can certifiably convey her information, she does so for good news, but unless this leads the principal to make a very costly mistake, to shelter his feelings she pretends to be uninformed when the news is bad.
papers.summary_en: A doctor decides whether to tell a patient bad news. A manager decides whether to hide a company problem from the board. In each case, a better-informed "agent" chooses what to do on behalf of a "principal" whose feelings the agent cares about. This paper models how the agent's desire to protect the principal's anticipatory utility distorts her decisions — she shifts instrumental choices toward actions associated with good news. When she can certify good news she does so; for bad news, she pretends. But the equilibrium outcome is inefficient: undistorted action would yield higher total utility. A clean framework for analysing paternalism and information disclosure.
papers.data_used: Theoretical paper; no empirical data.
papers.policy_relevance: Indirect but broad relevance to Hungarian public-sector communication and political economy. The model rationalises why policymakers systematically underweight bad news, why doctors soften medical disclosures, and why organisations filter internal information. Relevant to debates about informational transparency (e.g., disclosing full macroeconomic projections, auditing public projects). Caveat: theoretical; does not prescribe specific Hungarian interventions.

papers.title: Utility from anticipation and personal equilibrium
Behavioral Contract Theory
papers.abstract: This review provides a critical survey of psychology-and-economics (“behavioral-economics”) research in contract theory. First, I introduce the theories of individual decision making most frequently used in behavioral contract theory, and formally illustrate some of their implications in contracting settings. Second, I provide a more comprehensive (but informal) survey of the psychology-and-economics work on classical contract-theoretic topics: moral hazard, screening, mechanism design, and incomplete contracts. I also summarize research on a new topic spawned by psychology and economics, exploitative contracting, that studies contracts designed primarily to take advantage of agent mistakes. (JEL A12, D03, D82, D86)
papers.summary_en: A survey article that maps how psychology-and-economics ideas have entered contract theory. It covers standard topics (moral hazard, screening, mechanism design, incomplete contracts) and one new one: "exploitative contracting" — contracts designed to exploit predictable consumer mistakes. The review synthesises two decades of behavioural contract theory and identifies the most robust empirical patterns. It's the standard reference for anyone teaching or extending behavioural contract theory and for policymakers who want a textbook-style overview of the field.
papers.data_used: Survey paper; no original empirical data.
papers.policy_relevance: A foundational survey rather than a new policy analysis. Useful for Hungarian consumer-protection and financial-regulation policymakers as a framework for identifying which specific market practices are likely to be exploitative versus merely inefficient. Relevant to GVH, MNB, and NFH staff. Caveat: a survey synthesises; specific policy design requires turning its ideas into testable interventions for specific Hungarian markets.

papers.title: Drive and Talent
papers.journal: Journal of the European Economic Association
papers.abstract: Journal Article Drive and Talent Get access Botond Kőszegi, Botond Kőszegi 1University of California, Berkeley Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar Wei Li Wei Li 2University of California, Riverside Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar Journal of the European Economic Association, Volume 6, Issue 1, 1 March 2008, Pages 210–236, https://doi.org/10.1162/JEEA.2008.6.1.210 Published: 01 March 2008
papers.summary_en: Why do some talented people underperform and less-talented ones overperform? This paper models the interaction between "drive" (effort motivation) and "talent" (ability). When talent is uncertain, feedback-inducing investments reveal it, and agents with different motivational structures react differently. The result: drive and talent act as substitutes in some settings but complements in others. The paper helps explain when self-selection of the driven into demanding jobs leads to positive or negative correlation with observed ability. A contribution to the economics of career choice and motivation.
papers.data_used: Theoretical paper; no empirical data.
papers.policy_relevance: For Hungarian career-counselling policy, talent-management HR practice, and public-sector hiring. The paper highlights that selection based on past achievement aggregates drive and talent in complex ways; simple meritocratic rules can have unintended effects. Caveat: theoretical and somewhat abstract; operational Hungarian applications require empirical measurement of the drive-talent correlation in specific occupations.

papers.title: Fragile Self-Esteem
papers.abstract: Abstract We develop a model of fragile self-esteem—self-esteem that is vulnerable to objectively unjustified swings—and study its implications for choices that depend on, or are aimed at enhancing or protecting, one’s self-view. In our framework, a person’s self-esteem is determined by sampling his memories of ego-relevant outcomes in a fashion that in turn depends on how he feels about himself, potentially creating multiple fragile “self-esteem personal equilibria.” Self-esteem is especially likely to be fragile, as well as unrealistic in either the positive or the negative direction, if being successful is important to the agent. A person with a low self-view might exert less effort when success is more important. An individual with a high self-view, in contrast, might distort his choices to prevent a collapse in self-esteem, with the distortion being greater if his true ability is lower. We discuss the implications of our results for mental well-being, education, job search, workaholism, and aggression.
papers.summary_en: Some people have "fragile self-esteem" that swings on small cues — successes make them feel briefly great, failures lead to crashes. This paper models how fragile self-esteem arises. A person samples memories of ego-relevant outcomes, and how they sample depends on how they feel about themselves, creating multiple self-esteem equilibria. Self-esteem is especially fragile when success is important. The paper shows how fragile self-esteem distorts behaviour: low-esteem people exert less effort when success matters more; high-esteem people may distort choices to protect their self-view. A new framework for motivation, procrastination, and self-defeating behaviour.
papers.data_used: Theoretical paper; no empirical data.
papers.policy_relevance: Relevant to Hungarian educational policy and labour-market interventions for disadvantaged youth. The paper's implication: interventions for students or job-seekers with low self-confidence need to build resilient self-esteem, not just deliver information or opportunities. Relevant to KRTK research on Roma youth, to early-childhood education policy, and to adult re-training programs. Caveat: theoretical; specific programme design would need to be grounded in empirical measurement of which Hungarian populations have fragile self-esteem.

papers.title: Choice Simplification: A Theory of Mental Budgeting and Naive Diversification*
papers.abstract: Abstract We develop a theory of how an agent makes basic multiproduct consumption decisions in the presence of taste, consumption opportunity, and price shocks that are costly to attend to. We establish that the agent often simplifies her choices by restricting attention to a few important considerations, which depend on the decision at hand and affect her consumption patterns in specific ways. If the agent’s problem is to choose the consumption levels of many goods with different degrees of substitutability, then she may create mental budgets for more substitutable products (e.g., entertainment). In some situations, it is optimal to specify budgets in terms of consumption quantities, but when most products have an abundance of substitutes, specifying budgets in terms of nominal spending tends to be optimal. If the goods are complements, in contrast, then the agent may—consistent with naive diversification—choose a fixed, unconsidered mix of products. And if the agent’s problem is to choose one of multiple products to fulfill a given consumption need (e.g., for gasoline or a bed), then it is often optimal for her to allocate a fixed sum for the need.
papers.summary_en: How do real people juggle many consumption choices at once? This paper argues that when thinking is costly, people simplify by creating "mental budgets" — e.g., a monthly budget for entertainment. The paper builds a theory of which kinds of choices get budgeted and how. If products are substitutes (different restaurants), consumers often use quantity budgets; if there are many substitutes, spending budgets become more attractive. For complements (goods consumed together), the theory predicts "naive diversification" — splitting spending across alternatives without weighing them. The framework unifies several well-documented behavioural patterns under a simple cognitive-cost story.
papers.data_used: Theoretical paper; no empirical data.
papers.policy_relevance: Relevant to Hungarian consumer-finance regulation and pension-portfolio default design. The mental-budgeting framework explains why consumers stick to simple allocations and may not respond to fine-grained financial advice. For MNB retirement-product regulation and pension-fund default portfolios: the paper supports limiting choice complexity and designing defaults that reflect realistic cognitive costs rather than idealised portfolio optimisation. Caveat: the model is behavioural and stylised; it describes a tendency, not every household, and does not translate directly into quantitative Hungarian welfare estimates.

papers.title: A Model of Reference-Dependent Preferences
papers.abstract: We develop a model of reference-dependent preferences and loss aversion where "gain-loss utility" is derived from standard "consumption utility" and the reference point is determined endogenously by the economic environment. We assume that a person's reference point is her rational expectations held in the recent past about outcomes, which are determined in a personal equilibrium by the requirement that they must be consistent with optimal behavior given expectations. In deterministic environments, choices maximize consumption utility, but gain-loss utility influences behavior when there is uncertainty. Applying the model to consumer behavior, we show that willingness to pay for a good is increasing in the expected probability of purchase and in the expected prices conditional on purchase. In within-day labor-supply decisions, a worker is less likely to continue work if income earned thus far is unexpectedly high, but more likely to show up as well as continue work if expected income is high.
papers.summary_en: Consumers are loss-averse — but loss-averse relative to what? This paper, a pillar of modern behavioural economics, proposes that the reference point is determined by recent rational expectations about outcomes. The model explains phenomena that pure consumption utility cannot: willingness to pay rises when purchase is expected; small-stakes risk aversion is higher than large-stakes; a worker is less likely to keep working if the day's income has already exceeded expectations. The framework is tractable, generates clean predictions, and replaces ad-hoc reference points in earlier prospect-theory work.
papers.summary_hu: A fogyasztók veszteségkerülők — de mire vonatkozóan? A tanulmány, a modern viselkedés-közgazdaságtan egyik pillére, azt javasolja, hogy a referenciapontot a kimenetelekről szóló friss racionális várakozások határozzák meg. A modell olyan jelenségeket magyaráz meg, amelyeket a tiszta fogyasztási hasznosság nem képes: a fizetési hajlandóság nő, amikor a vásárlás várt; a kis tétű kockázatkerülés magasabb, mint a nagy tétűé; egy dolgozó kevésbé folytatja a munkát, ha a napi jövedelem már meghaladta a várakozásait. A keret kezelhető, tiszta előrejelzéseket termel, és lecseréli a korábbi prospekt-elméleti munkákban szereplő ad-hoc referenciapontokat.
papers.data_used: Theoretical paper; the authors cite a range of empirical studies — from endowment-effect experiments to taxi-driver labour supply — to motivate predictions.
papers.data_used_hu: Elméleti tanulmány; a szerzők több empirikus tanulmányt idéznek — az endowment-hatás kísérletektől a taxisofőri munkakínálatig — az előrejelzések motiválásához.
papers.policy_relevance: Foundational behavioural-economics reference. For Hungarian policy, its relevance is through the many behavioural mechanisms it underpins: default choices in pension contributions, energy-bill framing for efficiency policy, price-quote design for consumer credit at MNB supervisory level, and labour-supply responses to progressive taxation. Any Hungarian "nudge" initiative should take this framework as a starting point when setting defaults and reference values. The model is highly abstract; direct mapping to a specific regulation requires additional empirical work.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: Alapvető viselkedés-közgazdasági referencia. A magyar szakpolitika számára a relevanciája azokon a viselkedési mechanizmusokon át érvényesül, amelyek rá épülnek: alapbeállítások a nyugdíj-hozzájárulásokban, energia-számlás keretezés hatékonysági politikához, fogyasztói hitel árajánlati tervezés MNB-felügyelet szintjén, és munkakínálati válaszok a progresszív adóztatásra. Bármely magyar „nudge" kezdeményezésnek ezt a keretet kell kiindulópontnak vennie. A modell nagyon absztrakt; konkrét szabályozásra való leképezés további empirikus munkát igényel.

papers.title: Reference-Dependent Risk Attitudes
papers.abstract: We use Kőszegi and Rabin's (2006) model of reference-dependent utility, and an extension of it that applies to decisions with delayed consequences, to study preferences over monetary risk. Because our theory equates the reference point with recent probabilistic beliefs about outcomes, it predicts specific ways in which the environment influences attitudes toward modest-scale risk. It replicates “classical” prospect theory—including the prediction of distaste for insuring losses—when exposure to risk is a surprise, but implies first-order risk aversion when a risk, and the possibility of insuring it, are anticipated. A prior expectation to take on risk decreases aversion to both the anticipated and additional risk. For large-scale risk, the model allows for standard “consumption utility” to dominate reference-dependent “gain-loss utility,” generating nearly identical risk aversion across situations. (JEL D81)
papers.summary_en: Building on their 2006 reference-dependent model, the authors develop a theory of decisions with risk. A key twist: attitudes toward risk depend on whether the risk is a surprise or expected. An unexpected risk produces the classical prospect-theory pattern (people refuse actuarially fair insurance in the loss domain). But an expected risk — and the possibility of insuring it — brings first-order risk aversion. Anticipating risk-taking in advance reduces the aversion to both the anticipated and any additional risk. For large-scale risk, consumption utility dominates and risk attitudes look standard.
papers.summary_hu: A 2006-os referenciafüggő modelljükre építve a szerzők kockázatos döntések elméletét dolgozzák ki. Kulcs-fordulat: a kockázathoz való hozzáállás attól függ, hogy a kockázat meglepetés-e vagy várt. Egy váratlan kockázat a klasszikus prospekt-elméleti mintát termeli (az emberek visszautasítják az aktuáriusilag méltányos biztosítást veszteség-tartományban). De egy várt kockázat — és a biztosítás lehetősége — első rendű kockázatkerülést hoz. A kockázatvállalás előre várása csökkenti a kockázatkerülést mind a várt, mind a további kockázatokra. Nagy léptékű kockázatoknál a fogyasztási hasznosság dominál és a kockázati hozzáállás standardnak tűnik.
papers.data_used: Theoretical paper; no empirical data.
papers.data_used_hu: Elméleti tanulmány; nincs empirikus adat.
papers.policy_relevance: For Hungarian insurance-market regulation (MNB) and pension-scheme default design: the paper's prediction that expected vs unexpected framing produces different risk-taking is directly relevant. Mandatory or auto-enrolled pension plans should show different savings and risk-portfolio behaviour than opt-in plans, even at the same participation rate. For individual-insurance markets, how products are framed (as loss protection vs as planned investment) changes take-up. Abstract; specific product-design implications require experimental follow-up.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: A magyar biztosítási-piaci szabályozáshoz (MNB) és a nyugdíj-séma alapértelmezés tervezéshez: az előrejelzés, hogy a várt és váratlan keretezés különböző kockázatvállalást termel, közvetlenül releváns. A kötelező vagy automatikusan regisztrált nyugdíjtervekben különböző megtakarítási és kockázat-portfolió viselkedést kellene mutatniuk, mint az opt-in terveknek, még azonos részvételi arány mellett is. Egyéni biztosítási piacokra: a termékek keretezése (veszteség-védelemként vagy tervezett befektetésként) megváltoztatja az igénybevételt. Absztrakt; specifikus termék-tervezési következményekhez kísérleti utánkövetés kell.

papers.title: Choices, situations, and happiness
Revealed Mistakes and Revealed Preferences
papers.abstract: Because some are not able to assess all the possible options for making a decision of an action, people often defy one of the most fundamental and traditional concepts in economics by going for an option that they would not have chosen had they been able to push through with thorough evaluation. Because of this, it would be useful to first analyze the common mistakes made until they become uncommon and until we are able to integrate what we have learned from these mistakes into creating models, policy prescriptions, and evaluations. Although certain fears may arise from such a measure on both the positive and normative side through mistakes becoming a free parameter and because of how the revealed-preference approach cannot be used, this chapter suggests other measures to deal with such concerns.

papers.title: Reference-Dependent Consumption Plans
papers.abstract: We develop a rational dynamic model in which people are loss averse over changes in beliefs about present and future consumption. Because changes in wealth are news about future consumption, preferences over money are reference-dependent. If news resonates more when about imminent consumption than when about future consumption, a decision maker might (to generate pleasant surprises) overconsume early relative to the optimal committed plan, increase immediate consumption following surprise wealth increases, and delay decreasing consumption following surprise losses. Since higher wealth mitigates the effect of bad news, people exhibit an unambiguous first-order precautionary-savings motive. (JEL D14, D81, D83, D91)
papers.summary_en: The authors extend their reference-dependent model to intertemporal consumption-savings decisions. Consumers get utility from consumption, but also from the news they receive about future consumption. If news about imminent consumption matters more than news about distant consumption, then consumers may overconsume early (to generate pleasant surprises), spend more immediately when they get a surprise windfall, and delay reducing consumption after a surprise loss. A robust implication: people have a clear precautionary-savings motive because higher wealth buffers against future bad news.
papers.summary_hu: A szerzők a referencia-függő modellt az időbeli fogyasztás-megtakarítási döntésekre bővítik. A fogyasztók hasznosságot kapnak a fogyasztásból, de a jövőbeli fogyasztásról szóló hírekből is. Ha a közelebbi fogyasztásról szóló hírek jobban számítanak, mint a távoli fogyasztásról szólók, akkor a fogyasztók korán túlfogyaszthatnak (hogy kellemes meglepetéseket generáljanak), azonnal többet költenek egy meglepetésszerű többlettel, és elhalasztják a csökkentést egy meglepetésszerű veszteség után. Robusztus implikáció: az embereknek világos elővigyázatossági megtakarítási motívumuk van, mert a magasabb vagyon véd a jövőbeli rossz hírek ellen.
papers.data_used: Theoretical paper; no empirical data.
papers.data_used_hu: Elméleti tanulmány; nincs empirikus adat.
papers.policy_relevance: For Hungarian household-finance and pension policy: the model provides microfoundations for the observed "consumption smoothing" failure — households don't cut consumption as much as standard theory predicts after bad news. Relevant to MNB macroprudential and ÖNYP pension-fund design decisions. Suggests that public policy can improve welfare by shaping expectations (e.g., committing to gradual pension-benefit phase-ins). Conceptual; specific Hungarian implementation requires empirical calibration.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: Magyar háztartási-pénzügyi és nyugdíjpolitikához: a modell mikroalapokat ad a megfigyelt „fogyasztás-simítási" kudarcnak — a háztartások nem annyira csökkentik a fogyasztást, mint amennyit a standard elmélet előrejelez rossz hírek után. Releváns az MNB makroprudenciális és ÖNYP nyugdíjalap-tervezési döntéseihez. Azt sugallja, hogy a közpolitika a várakozások alakításával javíthat a jóléten (pl. a nyugdíj-juttatás fokozatos bevezetése). Fogalmi; konkrét magyar megvalósítás empirikus kalibrációt igényel.

papers.title: A Model of Focusing in Economic Choice*
papers.abstract: Abstract We present a generally applicable theory of focusing based on the hypothesis that a person focuses more on, and hence overweights, attributes in which her options differ more. Our model predicts that the decision maker is too prone to choose options with concentrated advantages relative to alternatives, but maximizes utility when the advantages and disadvantages of alternatives are equally concentrated. Applying our model to intertemporal choice, these results predict that a person exhibits present bias and time inconsistency when—such as in lifestyle choices and other widely invoked applications of hyperbolic discounting—the future effect of a current decision is distributed over many dates, and the effects of multiple decisions accumulate. But unlike in previous models, in our theory (1) present bias is lower when the costs of current misbehavior are less dispersed, helping explain why people respond more to monetary incentives than to health concerns in harmful consumption; and (2) time inconsistency is lower when a person commits to fewer decisions with accumulating effects in her ex ante choice. In addition, a person does not fully maximize welfare even when making decisions ex ante: (3) she commits to too much of an activity—for example, exercise or work—that is beneficial overall; and (4) makes “future-biased” commitments when—such as in preparing for a big event—the benefit of many periods’ effort is concentrated in a single goal.
papers.summary_en: People focus more on attributes where options differ more — and give those attributes excessive weight. This paper builds that insight into a formal model and explores consequences for intertemporal choice. Prediction: people exhibit present bias when the future consequences of today's choice are spread out over many dates (skipping the gym once has diffuse future health costs), but less bias when costs concentrate (a specific medical bill). The model explains why people respond more to monetary incentives than to health concerns and why commitment devices help in some contexts but not others.
papers.summary_hu: Az emberek azokra a szempontokra koncentrálnak jobban, ahol az opciók jobban eltérnek — és aránytalanul súlyt adnak ezeknek. A tanulmány formális modellbe építi ezt a meglátást, és az időbeli döntésekre vonatkozó következményeket tárja fel. Előrejelzés: az emberek jelenhajlamot mutatnak, amikor a mai döntés jövőbeli következményei sok dátumon át szórva vannak (egy edzés elhagyása szétterül a jövőbeli egészségi költségeken), de kevésbé elfogultak, amikor a költségek koncentráltak (egy konkrét orvosi számla). A modell megmagyarázza, miért reagálnak az emberek jobban pénzügyi ösztönzőkre, mint egészségügyi aggodalmakra, és miért segítenek az elkötelezés-eszközök egyes kontextusokban, míg másokban nem.
papers.data_used: Theoretical paper; illustrative applications to labour supply, health behaviour, and savings.
papers.data_used_hu: Elméleti tanulmány; illusztratív alkalmazások munkakínálatra, egészségi viselkedésre és megtakarításokra.
papers.policy_relevance: For Hungarian behavioural-policy design: provides a framework for deciding when nudges and commitments are likely to work. Works well for concentrated costs/benefits (tax deductions, specific medical screenings); less well when effects diffuse over time (lifetime smoking damage). Relevant for Ministry of Finance and Ministry of Interior-health-portfolio decisions on financial vs health-focused messaging for behaviour change. Abstract; informs the design of nudges rather than delivering a specific one.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: A magyar viselkedés-politika tervezéséhez: keretet ad annak eldöntéséhez, mikor valószínű, hogy a nudge-ok és az elkötelezések működnek. Jól működnek koncentrált költségekre/előnyökre (adólevonások, specifikus orvosi szűrések); kevésbé, amikor a hatások szétszóródnak időben (élethosszig tartó dohányzási ártalmak). Releváns a Pénzügyminisztérium és a Belügyminisztérium egészségügyi portfóliójának döntéseihez pénzügyi vagy egészség-fókuszú üzenetek viselkedés-változáshoz. Absztrakt; inkább a nudge-ok tervezéséről tájékoztat, mintsem konkrét javaslatot adna.

papers.title: Equilibrium Default Cycles
papers.abstract: This paper analyzes Markov equilibria in a model of strategic lending in which (i) agents cannot commit to long‐term contracts, (ii) contracts are incomplete, and (iii) incumbent lenders can coordinate their actions. Default cycles occur endogenously over time along every equilibrium path. After a sequence of bad shocks, the borrower in a competitive market accumulates debt so large that the incumbent lenders exercise monopoly power. Even though the incumbents could maintain this power forever, they find it profitable to let the borrower regain access to the competitive market after a sequence of good shocks. Equilibria are computed numerically, and their attributes are qualitatively consistent with numerous known empirical facts on sovereign lending. In addition, the model predicts that a borrower who accumulates debt overhang will regain access to the competitive credit market only after good shocks. This prediction is shown to be consistent with data on emerging market economies.
papers.summary_en: Sovereign-debt markets go through cycles of easy access and tight credit. This paper builds a model where the cycles are endogenous. Lenders can't commit to long-term contracts, contracts are incomplete, and incumbent lenders can coordinate among themselves. After bad shocks, the borrower's debt rises to the point where incumbents exercise monopoly power; after good shocks, incumbents let the borrower back into competitive markets because it pays to do so. Default cycles emerge without any exogenous shock to preferences or institutions. The model's implications match many known facts about sovereign-debt markets.
papers.data_used: Theoretical model calibrated and compared against empirical facts on sovereign-lending markets (spreads, debt concentration, default frequency).
papers.policy_relevance: For Hungarian sovereign-debt management (AKK, MNB): the paper supports active monitoring of incumbent-lender concentration during stress episodes. Diversification across lender types (retail domestic bonds, FX bonds, syndicated loans) is valuable precisely because it reduces incumbents' coordinated monopoly power. Relevant to Hungarian debt-management strategy. Caveat: the model is about developing-country sovereign lending in the 1970s-80s; Hungarian debt is issued largely in EU markets with different institutional features.

papers.title: Take-up and labor supply responses to disability insurance earnings limits
papers.abstract: In most disability insurance programs beneficiaries lose some or all of their benefits if they earn above an earnings threshold. While intended to screen out applicants with high remaining working capacity, earnings limits can also distort the labor supply of beneficiaries. We use a reduction in the earnings limit in Hungary to evaluate this trade-off and examine screening and labor supply responses. We find that the policy changed selection into the program modestly but reduced labor supply on the intensive margin significantly. These findings suggest that the earnings threshold should be higher.
papers.summary_en: Disability insurance has an earnings limit: if you earn above the threshold, you lose some benefits. This is designed to screen out people who can work. But it can also distort the labour supply of beneficiaries. This paper uses a 2012 Hungarian reform that reduced the earnings limit to evaluate the trade-off. The finding: the policy changed selection into disability only modestly, but reduced labour supply on the intensive margin substantially. The recommendation: the earnings threshold should be higher. A concrete empirical lesson for disability-insurance design.
papers.data_used: Hungarian administrative data on disability insurance recipients and their earnings around the 2012 earnings-limit reform. Before/after comparison with control group.
papers.policy_relevance: Directly relevant to Hungarian disability-insurance policy (MÁK, OEP). The paper supports raising the earnings limit to reduce labour-supply distortion without much cost in selection quality. Actionable recommendation for Ministry of Economy and social-policy design. Caveat: the 2012 reform was a specific change; effects of alternative calibrations (phase-out rather than cliff) might differ.

papers.title: The effects of a disability employment quota when compliance is cheaper than defiance
papers.abstract: Abstract This paper evaluates the effects of the Hungarian disability employment quota, which requires firms over a size threshold to employ individuals with disabilities or pay a non‐compliance tax. In 2010, the tax was raised from very low levels to 170% of the minimum wage cost associated with meeting the quota. We employ a regression discontinuity design on firm‐level data to estimate the effects of the policy and provide a lower‐bound estimate to account for the potential bias resulting from firms bunching below the threshold. Firms respond to the quota by hiring 0.24–0.29 additional workers with disabilities, with a lower bound estimate of 0.12–0.17. However, about two‐thirds of the quota is not fulfilled, which is puzzling as the tax is higher than the minimum wage cost of a worker with disabilities. Our model shows that high hiring costs associated with employing individuals with disabilities might be an important factor behind this anomaly. We test this hypothesis by showing that the effect is weaker in regions with a low share of individuals with disabilities, implying that without adequate policies targeting the removal of supply‐side barriers to the employment of people with disabilities, even strong demand‐side financial incentives cannot achieve their goals.

papers.title: The effects of a disability employment quota when compliance is cheaper than defiance
papers.abstract: Abstract This paper evaluates the effects of the Hungarian disability employment quota, which requires firms over a size threshold to employ individuals with disabilities or pay a non‐compliance tax. In 2010, the tax was raised from very low levels to 170% of the minimum wage cost associated with meeting the quota. We employ a regression discontinuity design on firm‐level data to estimate the effects of the policy and provide a lower‐bound estimate to account for the potential bias resulting from firms bunching below the threshold. Firms respond to the quota by hiring 0.24–0.29 additional workers with disabilities, with a lower bound estimate of 0.12–0.17. However, about two‐thirds of the quota is not fulfilled, which is puzzling as the tax is higher than the minimum wage cost of a worker with disabilities. Our model shows that high hiring costs associated with employing individuals with disabilities might be an important factor behind this anomaly. We test this hypothesis by showing that the effect is weaker in regions with a low share of individuals with disabilities, implying that without adequate policies targeting the removal of supply‐side barriers to the employment of people with disabilities, even strong demand‐side financial incentives cannot achieve their goals.
papers.summary_en: Hungary's disability employment quota requires firms above a size threshold to employ disabled workers or pay a non-compliance tax. In 2010, this tax was raised dramatically (to 170% of minimum-wage cost). This paper uses a regression discontinuity around the size threshold to evaluate the policy. Firms do hire more disabled workers (0.24–0.29 additional, lower bound 0.12–0.17), but two-thirds of the quota is still not fulfilled. The puzzle: the tax is higher than the wage cost. The authors' model suggests high non-wage hiring costs (accommodation, compliance) explain why firms pay the tax rather than hire. The policy is a textbook example of unintended limits.
papers.data_used: Hungarian firm-level administrative data covering the 2010 disability-employment-quota tax increase. Regression-discontinuity design exploiting the firm-size threshold above which the quota applies.
papers.policy_relevance: Directly relevant to Hungarian disability-policy design (EMMI, Ministry of Economy). The paper quantifies why the quota is under-complied-with: non-wage hiring costs are high. Policy implication: raising the tax alone is ineffective; complementary policies (subsidised accommodations, dedicated matching services, employer information) are needed. Relevant to continuing reform debates. Caveat: identified from 2010 reform; labour-market conditions have since tightened, which may have changed the trade-off.

papers.title: RETAKING IN HIGH STAKES EXAMS: IS LESS MORE?
papers.abstract: Abstract Placement, both in university and in the civil service, according to performance in competitive exams is the norm in much of the world. Repeat taking of such exams is common despite the private and social costs it imposes. We develop and estimate a structural model of exam retaking using data from Turkey's university placement exam. Limiting retaking results in all agents gaining ex ante and most gaining ex post. This result comes from a general equilibrium effect: Retakers crowd the market and impose negative spillovers on others by raising acceptance cutoffs.
papers.summary_en: High-stakes university-placement exams often let students retake them. Retaking is costly privately and imposes costs on other students via general-equilibrium effects: retakers crowd the market and raise acceptance cutoffs for everyone. This paper builds and structurally estimates a model of exam retaking using Turkey's university placement data. The key finding: limiting retaking makes all students better off ex ante, and most better off ex post. The gain comes from the general-equilibrium spillover, not from protecting individual students from their own choices. A case where less choice can be welfare-improving through network effects.
papers.data_used: Structural model estimated on Turkey's centralised university-placement exam micro-data: exam scores, retake decisions, placement outcomes.
papers.policy_relevance: Direct relevance to Hungarian university admission (felvételi). Retakes of érettségi are allowed with limited constraints; the paper suggests tighter caps could be Pareto-improving via the same spillover mechanism. Relevant to Oktatási Hivatal and to policy debates on exam-retake fees. Caveat: Turkey-calibrated; Hungarian quantitative effects would depend on the share of retakers and alternative options (rolling-entry universities, online programmes) available to students.

papers.title: Pareto Improvements in the Contest for College Admissions
papers.abstract: Abstract Many countries base college admissions on a centrally administered test. Students invest a great deal of resources to improve their performance on the test, and there is growing concern about the high costs associated with these activities. We consider modifying the test by introducing performance-disclosure policies that pool intervals of performance rankings. Pooling affects the equilibrium allocation of students to colleges, which hurts some students and benefits others, but also affects students’ effort. We investigate how such policies can improve students’ welfare in a Pareto sense, study the Pareto frontier of pooling policies, and identify improvements that are robust to the distribution of college seats. We illustrate the potential applicability of our results with an empirical estimation that uses data on college admissions in Turkey. We find that a policy that pools a large fraction of the lowest-performing students leads to a Pareto improvement in a contest based on the estimated parameters. A laboratory experiment based on the estimated parameters generally supports our theoretical predictions.
papers.summary_en: Many countries rank students for university admission using a single centrally-administered exam. Students invest heavily in test preparation, and there is growing concern about the private cost. This paper asks whether the "rat race" can be reduced without hurting anyone. The authors design pooling policies — a scoring scheme that lumps some performance brackets together — and show that these can make every student better off in equilibrium (a Pareto improvement). Illustrated with data from Turkey's centralised college-admissions exam: pooling the lowest-scoring group is the kind of change that would reduce private tutoring effort without degrading who goes to which university.
papers.data_used: Theoretical contest model with pooling policies, calibrated to illustrative data from Turkey's centralised university-admission exam (the OSYM/ÖSYM system): score distributions and seat allocations across universities.
papers.policy_relevance: Hungary's érettségi-based university admission is a high-stakes exam that drives heavy private investment in tutoring — a well-known source of inequality in who prepares and who does not. The paper offers a concrete design lever: pool performance brackets at the low end rather than publishing fine-grained rankings. This can reduce private tutoring effort without visibly changing who gets into top programmes. Relevant for Oktatási Hivatal (Education Office) and the ministry running the felsőoktatási felvételi. Caveat: the Turkish calibration is structure-specific; a Hungarian reform should be evaluated with Hungarian exam-score and choice data before roll-out.

papers.title: Learning and Price Discovery in a Search Market
papers.abstract: We introduce learning into an otherwise standard two-sided search-and-bargaining market. There is uncertainty about the price distribution due to uncertainty about an underlying exogenous state of relative demand: in the high state, buyers are on the long side; otherwise, they are on the short side. In equilibrium, prices are on average higher in the high state. Individual agents learn about the distribution while searching. Agents typically start out by experimenting with a tough bargaining position—buyers may initially insist on a low price and sellers on a high price. After successive failures to trade, agents become increasingly pessimistic about the market conditions and soften their bargaining stance. When frictions are small, equilibrium transaction prices are approximately market clearing, despite aggregate demand being unknown. Thus, the search-and-bargaining procedure enables price discovery in a decentralized market.
papers.summary_en: When you go to a market uncertain about whether you're on the long or short side, what price do you ask or offer? This paper introduces learning into a classic two-sided search-and-bargaining market. Agents don't know aggregate demand conditions and start out bargaining tough — buyers demanding low prices, sellers high. As trades fail, they soften their positions. When frictions are small, the market ultimately converges close to market-clearing prices even though nobody knows the aggregate state. A clean demonstration that decentralised bargaining can aggregate information about market fundamentals.
papers.data_used: Theoretical paper; no empirical data.
papers.policy_relevance: Foundational for market-design questions — when can decentralised trading aggregate information efficiently? Indirect policy relevance for Hungarian market-design contexts (energy markets, labour markets with tightness uncertainty). Caveat: theoretical; specific applications require mapping the model to institutional features of particular Hungarian markets.

papers.title: Auctions in Markets: Common Outside Options and the Continuation Value Effect
papers.abstract: In this paper, we study auctions with outside options provided by future market interaction focusing on the revenue effects of some information revelation policies. We show that auctions with less information revelation may yield higher revenues. In particular, we show that it is never optimal for the auctioneer to reveal information after the auction. Moreover, it is also not optimal to reveal information before the auction unless bidders already have precise information on their own. Our model provides a novel explanation for the prevalence of opaque trading mechanisms, and it offers insights into information sharing in dynamic models of trade. (JEL D44, D83)
papers.summary_en: When future market interaction provides outside options to bidders, what information should an auctioneer reveal? This paper shows that less information revelation can be revenue-maximising. Revealing information after the auction is never optimal. Revealing information before the auction is only optimal when bidders already have good private signals; otherwise the auctioneer benefits from opacity. The paper explains the prevalence of "dark pools" and other opaque trading mechanisms in securities markets, and suggests that information-sharing in repeat trading is less about trust than about auction revenue.
papers.summary_hu: Amikor a jövőbeli piaci interakció külső opciókat biztosít az ajánlattevőknek, milyen információt kellene az aukciórendezőnek felfedni? A tanulmány megmutatja, hogy kevesebb információfelfedés lehet bevétel-maximalizáló. Az aukció utáni információ felfedése soha nem optimális. Az aukció előtti információfelfedés csak akkor optimális, amikor az ajánlattevőknek már jó magán-jeleik vannak; egyébként az aukciórendező profitál az átláthatatlanságból. A tanulmány megmagyarázza az „sötét poolok" és más átláthatatlan kereskedési mechanizmusok elterjedtségét az értékpapír-piacokon, és azt sugallja, hogy az ismételt kereskedésben az információmegosztás kevésbé szól a bizalomról, mint az aukció-bevételről.
papers.data_used: Theoretical paper; no empirical data.
papers.data_used_hu: Elméleti tanulmány; nincs empirikus adat.
papers.policy_relevance: Relevant to financial-market regulation (MNB, MiFID II context) and public-auction design: the paper pushes back against the reflexive "more transparency is better" view. Some opacity can be revenue-maximising and even welfare-enhancing when traders have good private signals. Applies to how the State Treasury (ÁKK) designs government-bond auctions and how public asset disposals are structured. Abstract; detailed application to specific auctions requires market-design expertise.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: Releváns a pénzügyi-piaci szabályozáshoz (MNB, MiFID II-kontextus) és nyilvános aukció-tervezéshez: a tanulmány visszalép a reflexszerű „több átláthatóság jobb" nézettől. Egyes átláthatatlanság lehet bevétel-maximalizáló és akár jólét-növelő is, amikor a kereskedőknek jó magán-jelei vannak. Alkalmazható arra, hogyan tervezi az Állami Adósságkezelő (ÁKK) az államkötvény-aukciókat és hogyan struktúráljuk a közvagyon-értékesítéseket. Absztrakt; részletes alkalmazás specifikus aukciókra piactervezési szakértelmet igényel.

papers.title: The Adverse Effect of Information on Governance and Leverage
papers.abstract: We study the effect that internal information systems have on a firm’s leverage and corporate governance choices. Information systems lower governance costs by facilitating more targeted interventions. But they also generate asymmetric information between firms and their investors. As a result, firms may attempt to signal their superior quality by assuming more leverage. In some circumstances, this can reduce governance incentives and result in inferior outcomes. Investors anticipate this effect, and it renders information systems inefficient. The online appendix is available at https://doi.org/10.1287/mnsc.2016.2599 . This paper was accepted by Amit Seru, finance.

papers.title: Health Care Rationing in Public Insurance Programs: Evidence from Medicaid
papers.abstract: We study two mechanisms used by public health insurance programs for rationing health care: outsourcing to private managed care plans and quantity limits for prescription drugs. Leveraging a natural experiment in Texas’s Medicaid program, we find that the shift to managed care and the relaxation of a strict drug cap increased access to high-value drugs and outpatient services and reduced avoidable hospitalizations. Program costs increased significantly, indicating a trade-off between cost and quality. We provide suggestive evidence attributing the reduction in hospitalizations to the relaxation of the drug cap and much of the spending increase to the shift to managed care. (JEL G22, H75, I13, I18, I38)

papers.title: Noisy Contagion Without Mutation
papers.abstract: In a local interaction game agents play an identical stage game against their neighbours over time. For nearest neighbour interaction, it is established that, starting from a random initial configuration in which each agent has a positive probability of playing the risk dominant strategy, a sufficiently large population coordinates in the long-run on the risk dominant equilibrium almost surely. Our result improves on Blume (1995), Ellison (2000), and Morris (2000) by showing that the risk dominant equilibrium spreads to the entire population in a two dimensional lattice and without the help of mutation, as long as there is some randomness in the initial configuration.

papers.title: Competition, confidence and gender: Shifting the focus from the overconfident to the realistic
papers.abstract: The gender gap in competitiveness is argued to explain gender differences in later life outcomes, including career choices and the gender wage gap. In experimental settings, a prevalent explanation attributes this gap to males being more (over)confident than females (we call this the compositional channel). While our lab-in-the-field study using data from students in 53 classrooms (N > 1000) reproduces this finding, it also uncovers a second, potentially more impactful channel of confidence contributing to the gender gap in competitiveness (the preference channel). To disentangle the two channels, we propose a more precise measure of confidence based on whether the subjects’ believed performance rank exceeds, coincides with or falls short of their actual performance in a real-effort task. We label categories of this Guessed - Actual Performance (GAP) difference as overconfident, realistic or underconfident, respectively. Surprisingly, there is no gender difference in competitiveness within the over- and underconfident subgroups, while a significant gender gap exists among the realistic. So, even if both genders had the same level of confidence, a persistent gender gap in preference (or taste) for competition would remain in the realistic group. This finding is robust across all specifications, challenging previous theories about the overconfidence of men being the main driver of the relationship between confidence and the gender gap in competition.

papers.title: Regional Clustering Tendencies of the Hungarian Automotive and ICT Industries in the First Half of the 2000s
The geography of segregated online social networks in the largest US cities
papers.abstract: Cities are known for high levels of segregation that manifest in both online and offline environments. Far-reaching consequences of urban segregation include rising disparities, political polarization, and exposure to economic crises. In this chapter, we review the wide amount of literature on the emerging urban science discussion to provide an overview of modern data sources that can reveal the nature of segregation in cities. Our focus is on social interaction in urban spaces through mobility and social networks. Using geolocated Twitter messages in the 50 largest metropolitan areas in the US, we demonstrate how the data can quantify social networks around home and as a function of commuting to work. We argue that geotagged social media data enable us to better understand the spatial scale of segregation and the potential mitigation of inequalities through mixing and inclusion. The chapter presents the benefits and limitations of these data sources and discusses potential future research.
papers.summary_en: Cities are segregated both in who lives where and in who talks to whom online. This book chapter reviews recent urban-science work using geolocated social-media data to measure segregation at multiple spatial scales, and applies the framework to the 50 largest US metros using Twitter data. The chapter quantifies social-tie patterns around home and around workplaces, examining how commuting geography interacts with segregation. It argues that geotagged social-media data uniquely reveal the scale of urban segregation and can inform mixing-and-inclusion policies.
papers.summary_hu: A városok szegregáltak abban, ki hol lakik és ki kivel beszél online. Ez a könyvfejezet összefoglalja a legutóbbi város-tudományi munkát, amely geolokalizált közösségi-média-adatokat használ a szegregáció több térbeli skálán való mérésére, és alkalmazza a keretet az 50 legnagyobb amerikai metropoliszra Twitter-adatokkal. A fejezet kvantifikálja a társadalmi-kapcsolati mintákat otthon és munkahely körül, azt vizsgálva, hogyan lép kölcsönhatásba az ingázási földrajz a szegregációval. Érvel amellett, hogy a geotagelt közösségi-média-adatok egyedülállóan feltárják a városi szegregáció léptékét és tájékoztathatják a keveredés-és-befogadás politikáit.
papers.data_used: Geo-located Twitter data for the 50 largest US metropolitan areas, combined with home-address inference and commuting patterns; analysis period ~2010s.
papers.data_used_hu: Geolokalizált Twitter-adatok az 50 legnagyobb amerikai metropoliszra, kombinálva otthoni-cím következtetéssel és ingázási mintákkal; elemzési időszak ~2010-es évek.
papers.policy_relevance: For Hungarian urban-segregation monitoring (Budapest Municipality, Ministry of Interior): the chapter's methods could be adapted to Hungarian social-media data to reveal segregation patterns not visible in census data alone. Useful reference for thinking about the measurement problem. Setting is US; Hungarian application would require adapted data sources and privacy-compliant methods.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: A magyar város-szegregáció monitoringhoz (Budapest Önkormányzat, Belügyminisztérium): a fejezet módszerei adaptálhatók magyar közösségi-média-adatokhoz, hogy feltárják a cenzusadatokkal önmagában nem látható szegregációs mintákat. Hasznos referencia a mérési probléma gondolkodásához. A környezet amerikai; magyar alkalmazás adaptált adatforrásokat és adatvédelem-kompatibilis módszereket igényelne.

papers.title: Co-worker networks, labour mobility and productivity growth in regions
papers.abstract: The mobility of workers is an important source of regional dynamics, but the effect of mobility on regional productivity growth is not straightforward, as some firms tend to win while others lose from mobility. In the present paper, we argue that the co-worker networks across plants that are established by labour moves are important for both local learning opportunities and job matching quality and should hence facilitate regional growth. We therefore propose a new homophily-biased perspective on co-worker network creation and show that it suits geographical analyses better than random networks do. Moreover, panel vector autoregression models provide systematic evidence that an increase in co-worker network density is positively related to regional productivity growth. This is found to be important even when only ties across plants that are not directly linked by labour mobility are included.

papers.title: Az újraiparosodás térbeli kérdőjelei Magyarországon
papers.abstract: Az elmúlt két és fél évtizedben a hazai feldolgozóipar szervesen integrálódott a globális gazdaságba, egyre nagyobb teret nyert a külföldi működőtőke, megjelentek a modern iparágak, és jelentőssé vált az export. Ez a folyamat megtorpant 2008-ban, a válság generálta új kihívásokra adott gazdaságpolitikai válaszok között pedig megjelent az "újraiparosítás" fogalma. Tanulmányunkban azt vizsgáljuk, hogy a válság mélypontját követően, 2009 és 2014 között megfigyelhető-e újraiparosodás Magyarországon. Ha igen, akkor melyik térségben és iparágban, továbbá adottak-e a feltételek az újraiparosodás felgyorsításához? Vizsgálatainkból kiderül, hogy 2009-et követően az újraiparosodás csak néhány vidéki térségben érhető tetten, ott is csupán egy-két iparágban, míg például a fővárosban és vonzáskörzetében, valamint a nagy kutatóegyetemekkel rendelkező várostérségekben inkább az "ipartalanodás" bontakozik ki. A kevésbé fejlett kisvárosi térségek újraiparosodása lassú folyamat, amit helyalapú, alulról szerveződő, integrált gazdaságfejlesztéssel lehetne hatékonyabbá tenni. Journal of Economic Literature (JEL) kód: O14, L52, L60, O25, R58.

papers.title: Regional Innovation Systems in Hungary: The Failing Synergy at the National Level
papers.abstract: Abstract\n In this paper we use entropy statistics to measure the synergies of knowledge exploration, knowledge exploitation, and organizational control in Hungary. We categorized the firms in terms of sub-regions, industrial sectors, and firm size. Configurational information among these distributions is the indicator of the synergy in the system. The results indicate that three regimes were generated in the Hungarian transition period with different dynamics: Budapest emerges as a knowledge-based innovation system; in the north-western regions, foreign-owned companies have induced a shift in knowledge-organization; while the system is organized in the southern-eastern regions in accordance with government expenditures.

papers.title: Antidepressant use in spatial social networks
papers.abstract: Social networks may help individuals maintain their mental health. Most empirical work based on small-scale surveys finds that cohesive social networks are critical for mental well-being, while diverse networks are considered less important. Here, we link data on antidepressant use of 277,344 small-town residents to a nationwide online social network. The data enable us to examine how individuals' mental health care is related to the spatial characteristics of their social networks including their ties in the local community and connections to distant communities. We find that, besides the cohesion of social networks around home, the diversity of connections to distant places is negatively correlated with the probability of antidepressant use. Spatial diversity of social networks is also associated with decreasing dosage in subsequent years. This relationship is independent from the local access to antidepressants and is more prevalent for young individuals. Structural features of spatial social networks are prospectively associated with depression treatment.
papers.summary_en: Do your social networks protect your mental health? Small-scale surveys say yes — but only in narrow ways. This paper uses a nationwide dataset linking antidepressant purchases of 277,344 Hungarian small-town residents to the Hungarian online social-network structure. Beyond the standard finding that cohesive local networks matter, the authors show that spatial diversity in one's network (connections to distant places) is negatively correlated with antidepressant use and with dosage increases in subsequent years. The result is independent of local factors. A striking empirical connection between social-network geography and mental-health outcomes.
papers.summary_hu: Befolyásolja-e a szociális hálózatod a mentális egészségedet? 277 000 kisvárosi magyar lakos antidepresszáns-használatára vonatkozó adatokat egy országos online közösségi hálózattal összekapcsolva a tanulmány azt találja, hogy a szociális-hálózati diverzitás — nem csak a helyi kohézió — számít. Azok, akiknek távoli közösségekbe nyúló szociális kapcsolatai vannak, kevésbé valószínűen kezdenek antidepresszánst szedni és valószínűbben csökkentik az adagolást idővel. A hatás független a helyi gyógyszertári hozzáféréstől és legerősebb fiatalok körében. A tanulmány kihívást intéz a hagyományos nézettel szemben, miszerint csak a szoros, kohezív hálózatok támogatják a mentális jólétet — a távoli helyek felé nyúló „áthidaló" kapcsolatok is számítanak.
papers.data_used: Hungarian administrative data on antidepressant prescriptions (277,344 small-town residents) linked to a nationwide online social-network dataset. Individual-level analysis with spatial and network controls.
papers.data_used_hu: Magyar receptgyógyszer-(antidepresszáns-)rekordok 277 344 magyar kisvárosi lakosra, anonimizált országos online közösségi hálózattal összekapcsolva a kapcsolatok földrajzi kiterjedésével mért; többéves panel.
papers.policy_relevance: For Hungarian mental-health policy (EMMI, NEAK): the paper suggests that policies increasing rural-urban connectivity and mobility could have mental-health co-benefits. Relevant to regional-development programs with social-integration components. Caveat: correlation, not causation — unobserved selection (personality, life circumstances) may drive both network diversity and mental health. Cautious interpretation warranted.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: A Belügyminisztérium mentális egészségügyi portfóliójához és közegészségügyi ügynökségekhez: támogatja a hely-alapú mentális egészségügyi stratégiákat, amelyek a más közösségekbe való kapcsolódást mozdítják elő — pl. ifjúsági csereprogramokkal, migráció-támogatással vagy regionális mobilitási politikákkal. Konkrét bizonyíték arra, hogy a földrajzi elszigeteltségnek mentális egészségi költsége van a hagyományos meghatározókon túl. Magyar adat; azonnal alkalmazható. Figyelmeztetés: korrelációs — az oksági irány plauzibilis, de nem végérvényesen megállapított.

papers.title: An Alternative to Signaling: Directed Search and Substitution
papers.abstract: This paper analyzes a labor market, where: workers can acquire an observable skill at no cost, firms differ in unobserved productivity, workers' skill and firms' productivity are substitutes, and firms' search is directed. The main result is that, if the entry cost of firms is small, no worker acquires the skill in the unique equilibrium. For intermediate entry costs, a positive measure of workers obtain the skill, and the number of skilled workers goes to one as entry costs become large. Welfare is highest when the entry cost is high. (JEL D21, D24, D82, D83, J24)
papers.summary_en: Classical models argue education is valuable to workers partly as a costless signal of ability. This paper offers a different mechanism without signalling. Workers can observably acquire a skill; firms differ in unobserved productivity and search directedly; skill and firm productivity are substitutes. The main finding is that if firm entry is cheap, nobody acquires the skill in equilibrium. At intermediate entry costs, some workers acquire it; as entry costs rise, nearly all do. Welfare is highest when entry costs are high. The paper reframes when human-capital investment is socially valuable.
papers.data_used: Theoretical paper; no empirical data.
papers.policy_relevance: Offers a theoretical lens on Hungarian labour-market mismatch and the economics of adult-education/requalification programmes. Caveat: theoretical; concrete Hungarian policy recommendations depend on empirical identification of the substitutability assumption between worker skill and firm productivity, which varies by sector.

papers.title: The Use of Machine Learning in Treatment Effect Estimation
papers.journal: Advanced studies in theoretical and applied econometrics

papers.title: Optimal Binary Prediction for Group Decision Making
papers.journal: Journal of Business and Economic Statistics
papers.abstract: In this paper we address the problem of optimally forecasting a binary variable for a het-erogeneous group of decision makers facing various (binary) decision problems that are only tied together by the unknown outcome. A typical example is a weather forecaster who needs to estimate the probability of rain tomorrow and then report it to the public. Given a conditional probability model for the outcome of interest (e.g., logit or probit), we introduce the idea of maximum welfare estimation and derive conditions under which traditional estimators such as maximum likelihood or (non-linear) least squares are asymptotically socially optimal even when the underlying model is misspecified.

papers.title: On the Possibility of Informative Equilibria in Futures Markets with Feedback
papers.journal: Journal of the European Economic Association
papers.abstract: Abstract We study the existence of equilibria and the information content of prices in futures markets where the probability of future payoffs can be altered by an intervening agent who acts in response to the market price, hence creating a feedback effect. We focus on the market with the simplest possible structure: traders betting on the occurrence of a future event by buying or selling Arrow-Debreu securities (one dollar claims contingent on a binary outcome). We find that in the presence of feedback: (i) a rational expectations equilibrium may not exist; (ii) the market price may decline in response to information that is ex-ante more favorable to the occurrence of the underlying event; (iii) an equilibrium that reveals no information may obtain. Thus, feedback from an intervening agent materially alters the way in which price responds to information, and potentially undermines the viability of the market itself.
papers.summary_en: Futures markets are supposed to aggregate information — predicting elections, rainfall, or sport outcomes. But what if the outcome depends on an intervening agent (a central bank, a regulator) who watches the futures price and acts on it? That creates a feedback loop. This paper studies how feedback affects equilibria. Three surprising results: rational-expectations equilibria may not exist; the price can fall in response to ex-ante favourable news; a no-information-revealing equilibrium can arise. A caution that information-aggregation through prediction markets breaks when the outcome is endogenous to the price.
papers.data_used: Theoretical paper; no empirical data.
papers.policy_relevance: Relevant to debates about prediction markets as information tools for policy (election markets, climate markets, monetary-policy expectations). For MNB's use of market-implied policy expectations as a gauge of its own future actions: the paper warns that this signal can be degraded when the central bank reacts to it, a classic Goodhart problem dressed up as feedback. Caveat: theoretical, with clean cases; empirically the degree of feedback and how much it degrades information varies by setting.

papers.title: Closing the Gap between Risk Estimation and Decision Making: Efficient Management of Trade-Related Invasive Species Risk
papers.journal: The Review of Economics and Statistics
papers.abstract: This paper examines the implications of a binary action, binary outcome decision problem for estimating risk. We use data on the invasiveness of biological imports to develop the first comparison of two classical methods—maximum likelihood and Bayesian—against a third, the recently developed maximum utility (MU) approach. MU estimation uniquely takes advantage of the structure of the decision problem, which depends on a local rather than global fit to the model. Extending methods to account for an endogenously stratified sample, we show that the MU approach is less sensitive to specification error and can offer significant economic gains under model uncertainty.
papers.summary_en: When we estimate a risk model to inform a binary decision (accept/reject a biological import for risk of invasiveness), how should we estimate the model? This paper argues: not by maximum likelihood, which minimises global error. Instead, a "maximum utility" (MU) approach that focuses on the local region of the model most relevant to the decision can do much better — less sensitive to specification error and significantly higher economic gains under model uncertainty. Applied to biological-import data on invasive species. A valuable methodological contribution to decision-focused statistical estimation.
papers.data_used: Data on biological imports and their invasiveness outcomes. Comparison of MLE, Bayesian, and maximum-utility estimation approaches.
papers.policy_relevance: Relevant to Hungarian risk-assessment policy in any binary-decision context: environmental impact assessments, food-safety screening, biosecurity (agricultural-import controls, EU phytosanitary compliance). The methodological message — estimate for the decision, not the model — generalises well. Relevant to NAK (chamber of agriculture), NÉBIH (food safety authority). Caveat: the MU approach requires specifying the decision-utility structure carefully; it's not a drop-in replacement for MLE without thoughtful problem framing.

papers.title: ON THE RECOVERABILITY OF FORECASTERS’ PREFERENCES
papers.abstract: We study the problem of identifying a forecaster’s loss function from observations on forecasts, realizations, and the forecaster’s information set. Essentially different loss functions can lead to the same forecasts in all situations, though within the class of all continuous loss functions, this is strongly nongeneric. With the small set of exceptional cases ruled out, generic nonparametric preference recovery is theoretically possible, but identification depends critically on the amount of variation in the conditional distributions of the process being forecast. There exist processes with sufficient variability to guarantee identification, and much of this variation is also necessary for a process to have universal identifying power. We also briefly address the case in which the econometrician does not fully observe the conditional distributions used by the forecaster, and in this context we provide a practically useful set identification result for loss functions used in forecasting binary variables.

papers.title: Unrestricted and controlled identification of loss functions: Possibility and impossibility results
The construction of empirical credit scoring rules based on maximization principles
Using Multiple Outcomes to Adjust Standard Errors for Spatial Correlation
Policy Diffusion and Polarization across U.S. States
papers.abstract: Abstract Economists have studied the impact of numerous state laws, from welfare rules to voting ID requirements. Yet for all this policy evaluation, what do we know about policy diffusion—how these policies are introduced and spread from state to state? We present a series of facts based on a data set of 602 U.S. state policies spanning the past seven decades. First, proxies of state capacity do not predict a higher likelihood of innovating new policies, but the political leaning of the state does predict a higher likelihood of introducing partisan laws since 1990. Second, the diffusion of policies from 1950 to 2000 is best predicted by proximity—a state is more likely to adopt a policy if nearby states have already done so—as well as similarity in voter policy preferences. Third, since 2000, party alignment has become the strongest predictor of diffusion, and the speed of adoption has increased.
